questions.question: What is the minimum clearance distance between an aircraft on an aircraft stand and any adjacent building, aircraft on another stand, or other object for code letter F?
questions.explanation: Section 3.13.6 table: Code letter F clearance is 7.5 m.

questions.question: For a runway width of 45 m, how many stripes are required in the threshold marking according to the standard pattern?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4.5 table: Runway width 45 m corresponds to 12 stripes.

questions.question: What colour are taxiway markings?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.1.5: 'Taxiway markings, runway turn pad markings and aircraft stand markings shall be yellow.'

questions.question: What is the colour of runway markings?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.1.4: 'Runway markings shall be white.'

questions.question: True or False: The aiming point marking on a paved instrument runway with code number 2 shall commence no closer to the threshold than 250 m.
questions.explanation: Table 5-1: For landing distance available 800 m up to but not including 1 200 m, distance from threshold to beginning of marking is 250 m. Code number 2 runway typically has landing distance in that range.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a mandatory instruction sign?

options.A: To provide directional information to pilots
options.B: To identify a location beyond which an aircraft taxiing or vehicle shall not proceed unless authorized by the aerodrome control tower
options.C: To indicate the distance to the runway end

questions.explanation: Section 5.4.2.1: 'A mandatory instruction sign shall be provided to identify a location beyond which an aircraft taxiing or vehicle shall not proceed unless authorized by the aerodrome control tower.'

questions.question: What colour is the background of a mandatory instruction sign?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4.2.12: 'A mandatory instruction sign shall consist of an inscription in white on a red background.'

questions.question: What is the flash frequency for an aerodrome beacon?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.3.6: 'The frequency of total flashes shall be from 20 to 30 per minute.'

questions.question: What colour do identification beacons show at a land aerodrome?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.3.12: 'An identification beacon shall show flashing-green at a land aerodrome and flashing-yellow at a water aerodrome.'

questions.question: For a precision approach category I lighting system, what is the recommended length of the centre line row of lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.4.10: 'A precision approach category I lighting system shall consist of a row of lights on the extended centre line of the runway extending, wherever possible, over a distance of 900 m from the runway threshold...'

questions.question: What is the colour of runway threshold lights in the direction of approach?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.10.9: 'Runway threshold and wing bar lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing green in the direction of approach to the runway.'

questions.question: True or False: Runway centre line lights on a precision approach runway category II or III must show red from the threshold to the runway end.
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.12.7: 'Runway centre line lights shall be fixed lights showing variable white from the threshold to the point 900 m from the runway end; alternate red and variable white from 900 m to 300 m from the runway end; and red from 300 m to the runway end.'

questions.question: What is the colour of taxiway edge lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.18.7: 'Taxiway edge lights shall be fixed lights showing blue.'

questions.question: When must a stop bar be provided at a runway-holding position?

options.B: When the runway is intended to be used in runway visual range conditions less than 550 m, with exceptions
options.F: When the runway is used for take-off only

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.20.1: 'A stop bar shall be provided at every runway-holding position serving a runway when it is intended that the runway will be used in runway visual range conditions less than a value of 550 m except where: a) appropriate aids and procedures are available to assist in preventing inadvertent incursions ... or b) operational procedures exist to limit ...'

questions.question: True or False: Runway guard lights Configuration A consist of three pairs of yellow lights.
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.23.7: 'Runway guard lights, Configuration A, shall consist of two pairs of yellow lights.'

questions.question: What is the minimum length of a wind direction indicator?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.1.3: 'The wind direction indicator should be in the form of a truncated cone made of fabric and should have a length of not less than 3.6 m...'

questions.question: What colour is a landing 'T' indicator recommended to be?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.2.3: 'The colour of the landing 'T' shall be either white or orange, the choice being dependent on the colour that contrasts best with the background...'

questions.question: What is the minimum intensity of a signalling lamp intended for daytime use?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.3.3: 'When the signalling lamp is intended for use in the daytime the intensity of the coloured light should be not less than 6000 cd.'

questions.question: What are the minimum dimensions of a signal area?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.4.2: 'The signal area shall be an even horizontal surface at least 9 m square.'

questions.question: For a precision approach category II or III runway, what is the minimum width of the runway centre line marking stripes?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.3.4: 'The width of the stripes shall be not less than: 0.90 m on precision approach category II and III runways;'

questions.question: When a threshold is permanently displaced, what marking is required on the portion before the displaced threshold?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4.9: 'Where a runway threshold is permanently displaced, arrows conforming to Figure 5-4 (B) shall be provided on the portion of the runway before the displaced threshold.'
questions.image_description: Figure 5-4 (B) shows displaced threshold markings with arrows

questions.question: What is the recommended lateral spacing between the inner sides of the aiming point marking stripes for a runway with landing distance available 2400 m or above?
questions.explanation: Table 5-1: For landing distance available 2 400 m and above, lateral spacing between inner sides of stripes is 18–22.5 m.

questions.question: How many pairs of touchdown zone markings are required when the landing distance available is 2400 m or more?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.6.3 table: For landing distance available 2 400 m or more, number of pairs of markings is 6.

questions.question: What is the colour of taxiway centre line marking?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.1.5: 'Taxiway markings, runway turn pad markings and aircraft stand markings shall be yellow.' This includes centre line markings.

questions.question: What is the minimum width of a taxiway centre line marking?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.8.10: 'A taxiway centre line marking shall be at least 15 cm in width and continuous in length...'

questions.question: What is the pattern of a runway-holding position marking at an intersection of a taxiway and a non-instrument runway?

options.A: Pattern A (two solid lines, two dashed lines)

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.10.2: 'At an intersection of a taxiway and a non-instrument, non-precision approach or take-off runway, the runway-holding position marking shall be as shown in Figure 5-6, pattern A.'
questions.image_description: Figure 5-6, pattern A

questions.question: What is the colour of a VOR aerodrome checkpoint marking?

options.B: White (preferably) but should differ from taxiway marking colour

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.12.6: 'A VOR aerodrome checkpoint marking should preferably be white in colour but should differ from the colour used for the taxiway markings.'

questions.question: What is the recommended character height for mandatory instruction markings where code letter is C, D, E or F?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.16.9: 'The character height should be 4 m for inscriptions where the code letter is C, D, E or F, and 2 m where the code letter is A or B.'

questions.question: What colour are information markings that replace or supplement a location sign?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.17.6 a): 'an inscription in yellow upon a black background, when it replaces or supplements a location sign;'

questions.question: True or False: Elevated approach lights and their supporting structures must be frangible only up to 300 m from the threshold; beyond that, no frangibility requirement applies.
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.1.4: 'Elevated approach lights and their supporting structures shall be frangible except that, in that portion of the approach lighting system beyond 300 m from the threshold: a) where the height of a supporting structure exceeds 12 m, the frangibility requirement shall apply to the top 12 m only; and b) where a supporting structure is surrounded by non-frangible objects, only that part of the structure that extends above the surrounding objects shall be frangible.' So frangibility does apply, but limited.

questions.question: What is the maximum temperature that the interface between an installed inset light and an aircraft tire should not exceed during a 10-minute period of exposure?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.1.8: 'The temperature produced by conduction or radiation at the interface between an installed inset light and an aircraft tire should not exceed 160°C during a 10-minute period of exposure.'

questions.question: What is the minimum effective intensity of an aerodrome beacon flash?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.3.7: 'the effective intensity of the flash shall be not less than 2000 cd.'

questions.question: For a simple approach lighting system serving a non-precision approach runway, what is the recommended distance from threshold for the crossbar?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.4.2: 'A simple approach lighting system shall consist of a row of lights on the extended centre line of the runway extending, whenever possible, over a distance of not less than 420 m from the threshold with a row of lights forming a crossbar 18 m or 30 m in length at a distance of 300 m from the threshold.'

questions.question: What is the longitudinal interval for centre line lights in a precision approach category I lighting system?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.4.12: 'The lights forming the centre line shall be placed at longitudinal intervals of 30 m with the innermost light located 30 m from the threshold.'

questions.question: In a precision approach category II and III lighting system, what colour are the side row lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.4.36: 'The side row shall consist of barrettes showing red.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 5, what is the standard system recommended to be discontinued as of 1 January 2020?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.4: 'As of 1 January 2020, the use of T-VASIS and AT-VASIS as standard visual approach slope indicator systems should be discontinued.'

questions.question: In a PAPI system, how many light units does the wing bar consist of?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.24: 'The PAPI system shall consist of a wing bar of four sharp transition multi-lamp (or paired single lamp) units equally spaced.'

questions.question: What is the recommended obstacle protection surface slope for a PAPI system on a non-instrument runway with code number 1?
questions.explanation: Table 5-3: For non-instrument runways code number 1, PAPI slope is 'A - 0.57°' (where A is the approach slope angle).

questions.question: True or False: Runway threshold identification lights should be flashing white with a flash frequency between 60 and 120 per minute.
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.8.3: 'Runway threshold identification lights should be flashing white lights with a flash frequency between 60 and 120 per minute.'

questions.question: What is the maximum spacing for runway edge lights on an instrument runway?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.9.6: 'The lights shall be uniformly spaced in rows at intervals of not more than 60 m for an instrument runway, and at intervals of not more than 100 m for a non-instrument runway.'

questions.question: What colour do runway edge lights show in the approach direction at a displaced threshold?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.9.7 a): 'in the case of a displaced threshold, the lights between the beginning of the runway and the displaced threshold shall show red in the approach direction;'

questions.question: For a precision approach runway category II or III, what is the maximum spacing between threshold lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.10.4 c): 'lights uniformly spaced between the rows of runway edge lights at intervals of not more than 3 m.'

questions.question: What colour are runway threshold wing bar lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.10.9: 'Runway threshold and wing bar lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing green in the direction of approach to the runway.'

questions.question: What is the colour of runway end lights in the direction of the runway?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.11.4: 'Runway end lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing red in the direction of the runway.'

questions.question: What is the longitudinal spacing of runway centre line lights on a precision approach runway category II or III?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.12.5: 'The lights shall be located from the threshold to the end at longitudinal spacing of approximately 15 m. Where the serviceability level ... can be demonstrated and the runway is intended for use in runway visual range conditions of 350 m or greater, the longitudinal spacing may be approximately 30 m.'

questions.question: What is the colour of runway centre line lights from 300 m to the runway end?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.12.7: 'runway centre line lights shall be fixed lights showing ... red from 300 m to the runway end.'

questions.question: How far from the threshold do touchdown zone lights extend?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.13.2: 'Touchdown zone lights shall extend from the threshold for a longitudinal distance of 900 m, except that, on runways less than 1800 m in length, the system shall be shortened so that it does not extend beyond the midpoint of the runway.'

questions.question: What is the colour of simple touchdown zone lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.14.4: 'Simple touchdown zone lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing variable white, aligned so as to be visible to the pilot of a landing aeroplane in the direction of approach to the runway.'

questions.question: What colour are rapid exit taxiway indicator lights (RETILs)?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.15.5: 'Rapid exit taxiway indicator lights shall be fixed unidirectional yellow lights...'

questions.question: What colour are stopway lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.16.3: 'Stopway lights shall be fixed unidirectional lights showing red in the direction of the runway.'

questions.question: What is the colour of taxiway centre line lights on a taxiway other than an exit taxiway?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.17.6: 'taxiway centre line lights on a taxiway other than an exit taxiway ... shall be fixed lights showing green...'

questions.question: On an exit taxiway, what is the colour of the first taxiway centre line light from the runway centre line?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.17.7: 'The first light in the exit centre line shall always show green, and the light nearest to the perimeter shall always show yellow.'

questions.question: What colour are taxiway edge lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.18.7: 'Taxiway edge lights shall be fixed lights showing blue.'

questions.question: What is the minimum intensity of taxiway edge lights from 0° to 6° vertical?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.18.8: 'The intensity of taxiway edge lights shall be at least 2 cd from 0° to 6° vertical, and 0.2 cd at any vertical angles between 6° and 75°.'

questions.question: What colour are runway turn pad lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.19.6: 'Runway turn pad lights shall be unidirectional fixed lights showing green...'

questions.question: What is the maximum spacing for lights in a stop bar across a taxiway?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.20.5: 'Stop bars shall consist of lights spaced at uniform intervals of no more than 3 m across the taxiway, showing red in the intended direction(s) of approach...'

questions.question: When a stop bar is illuminated, what must happen to taxiway centre line lights beyond the stop bar?

options.C: They are extinguished for a distance of at least 90 m

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.20.12 c): 'when a stop bar is illuminated, any taxiway centre line lights installed beyond the stop bar shall be extinguished for a distance of at least 90 m;'

questions.question: What colour are intermediate holding position lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.21.4: 'Intermediate holding position lights shall consist of three fixed unidirectional lights showing yellow in the direction of approach to the intermediate holding position...'

questions.question: What colour are de-icing/anti-icing facility exit lights?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.22.3: 'De-icing/anti-icing facility exit lights shall consist of in-pavement fixed unidirectional lights spaced at intervals of 6 m showing yellow...'

questions.question: True or False: Runway guard lights Configuration A consist of two pairs of yellow lights.
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.23.7: 'Runway guard lights, Configuration A, shall consist of two pairs of yellow lights.'

questions.question: What is the recommended average horizontal illuminance on an aircraft stand?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.24.4: 'The average illuminance should be at least the following: Aircraft stand: horizontal illuminance 20 lux with a uniformity ratio (average to minimum) of not more than 4 to 1;'

questions.question: What two types of guidance must a visual docking guidance system provide?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.25.2: 'The system shall provide both azimuth and stopping guidance.'

questions.question: In an advanced visual docking guidance system (A-VDGS), what colour word should be displayed when an immediate cessation of docking is required?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.26.16: 'The word 'stop' in red characters should be displayed when an immediate cessation of the docking manoeuvre is required.'

questions.question: What colour are aircraft stand manoeuvring guidance lights (other than stop position)?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.27.3: 'Aircraft stand manoeuvring guidance lights, other than those indicating a stop position, shall be fixed yellow lights...'

questions.question: What is the flash frequency for a flashing-red road-holding position light?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.28.7: 'The flash frequency of the flashing-red light shall be between 30 and 60 flashes per minute.'

questions.question: Where should a no-entry bar be located?

options.A: At the threshold of a runway
options.B: Across a taxiway at the end of an exit only taxiway to prevent wrong-way entry
options.C: At the entrance to the apron
options.F: At the fuel station access road

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.29.2: 'A no-entry bar should be located across the taxiway at the end of an exit only taxiway where it is desired to prevent traffic from entering the taxiway in the wrong direction.'

questions.question: What colour are runway entrance lights (RELs) in a runway status lights system?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.30.4: 'RELs shall consist of a single line of fixed in pavement lights showing red in the direction of aircraft approaching the runway.'

questions.question: What colour is the background of a direction sign?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4.3.25: 'An information sign other than a location sign shall consist of an inscription in black on a yellow background.' Direction signs are information signs.

questions.question: What is the recommended character height for an aerodrome identification sign?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4.5.5: 'The characters should have a height of not less than 3 m.'

questions.question: What is the colour of a taxiway centre line marker?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.6.5: 'A taxiway centre line marker shall be retroreflective green.'

questions.question: What colour is a taxiway edge marker?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.5.3: 'A taxiway edge marker shall be retroreflective blue.'

questions.question: True or False: The obstacle protection surface for a visual approach slope indicator system has a total length of 15 000 m for instrument runways code number 3 or 4.
questions.explanation: Table 5-3: For instrument runways code number 3 or 4, total length is 15 000 m (for PAPI and T-VASIS/AT-VASIS where applicable).

questions.question: According to Chapter 5, what is the purpose of a wind direction indicator?

options.B: To give a clear indication of the direction of the surface wind and a general indication of the wind speed

questions.explanation: Section 5.1.1.3: '...constructed so that it gives a clear indication of the direction of the surface wind and a general indication of the wind speed.'

questions.question: What is the minimum length of a signal area?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.4.2: 'The signal area shall be an even horizontal surface at least 9 m square.' So minimum side length is 9 m.

questions.question: What is the colour of the landing 'T' when required for use at night?

options.B: Either illuminated or outlined by white lights

questions.explanation: Section 5.1.2.3: 'Where required for use at night the landing 'T' shall either be illuminated or outlined by white lights.'

questions.question: What is the minimum colour intensity required for a signalling lamp for daytime use?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.3.3: 'When the signalling lamp is intended for use in the daytime the intensity of the coloured light should be not less than 6000 cd.'

questions.question: True or False: Runway designation numbers on a single runway are determined by the nearest one-tenth of the magnetic North when viewed from the direction of approach.
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.2.4: 'On a single runway, dual parallel runways and triple parallel runways the two-digit number shall be the whole number nearest the one-tenth of the magnetic North when viewed from the direction of approach.'

questions.question: For a runway with a displaced threshold, how many stripes are required on each side of the centre line of the runway if the runway designation marking is placed within the threshold marking?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4.6: 'Where a runway designation marking is placed within a threshold marking there shall be a minimum of three stripes on each side of the centre line of the runway.'

questions.question: What is the distance from the threshold to the beginning of the aiming point marking for a runway with landing distance available less than 800 m?
questions.explanation: Table 5-1: For landing distance available less than 800 m, distance from threshold to beginning of marking is 150 m.

questions.question: For a paved precision approach runway with code number 2, what is the required minimum number of pairs of touchdown zone markings if the landing distance available is 900 m up to but not including 1 200 m?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.6.3 table: For landing distance available 900 m up to but not including 1 200 m, pair(s) of markings = 2.

questions.question: What is the colour of the mandatory instruction marking inscription?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.16.6: 'A mandatory instruction marking shall consist of an inscription in white on a red background.'

questions.question: What is the minimum viewing area for a taxiway edge marker as viewed by the pilot (recommended)?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.5.4: 'The marked surface as viewed by the pilot should be a rectangle and should have a minimum viewing area of 150 cm².'

questions.question: What is the recommended height of boundary markers in the form of a cone?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.8.3: '...or in the form of a cone not less than 50 cm high and not less than 75 cm in diameter at the base.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 5, what is the minimum number of lights in a threshold wing bar?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.10.8: 'Each wing bar shall be formed by at least five lights extending at least 10 m outward from, and at right angles to, the line of the runway edge lights...'

questions.question: For a taxiway intended for use in RVR conditions less than 350 m, what is the maximum longitudinal spacing of taxiway centre line lights on a straight section?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.17.13 c): 'on a taxiway intended for use in RVR conditions of less than a value of 350 m, the longitudinal spacing should not exceed 15 m.'

questions.sub_chapter: 6.1 Objects to be marked and/or lighted
questions.question: What is the minimum height of an obstacle that requires marking and lighting according to Annex 14?

options.B: Only those that penetrate obstacle limitation surfaces

questions.explanation: Section 6.1.1 specifies various conditions for marking and lighting obstacles, primarily those that extend above obstacle limitation surfaces (approach, transitional, horizontal, etc.). There is no universal minimum height; it depends on penetration of surfaces.

questions.question: What type of obstacle light is recommended for follow-me vehicles?
questions.explanation: Section 6.2.2.7: 'Low-intensity obstacle lights, Type D, shall be displayed on follow-me vehicles.'

questions.question: True or False: Low-intensity obstacle lights, Type C, displayed on vehicles associated with emergency or security shall be flashing-yellow.
questions.explanation: Section 6.2.2.6: 'Low-intensity obstacle lights, Type C, displayed on vehicles associated with emergency or security shall be flashing-blue and those displayed on other vehicles shall be flashing-yellow.'

questions.question: What is the recommended peak intensity for medium-intensity obstacle lights, Type A, during daytime (background luminescence above 500 cd/m²)?
questions.explanation: Table 6-1: Medium-intensity Type A, white flashing, peak intensity at given Background Luminescence: Day (Above 500 cd/m²) = 20 000 cd.

questions.question: For a chimney or other structure of like function, where should the top obstacle lights be placed?

options.A: At the very top of the structure
options.B: Sufficiently below the top to minimize contamination by smoke
options.E: On the ground surrounding the structure

questions.explanation: Section 6.2.3.11: 'In the case of chimney or other structure of like function, the top lights should be placed sufficiently below the top so as to minimize contamination by smoke, etc. (See Figure 6-2).'

questions.question: What is the recommended spacing for low-intensity obstacle lights on an extensive object?
questions.explanation: Section 6.2.3.15 a): 'where low-intensity lights are used, they shall be spaced at longitudinal intervals not exceeding 45 m;'

questions.question: What is the recommended overall colour for wind turbine rotor blades, nacelle, and upper 2/3 of the supporting mast?
questions.explanation: Section 6.2.4.2: 'The rotor blades, nacelle and upper 2/3 of the supporting mast of wind turbine should be painted white, unless otherwise indicated by an aeronautical study.'

questions.question: What is the recommended shape and minimum diameter for a marker displayed on an overhead wire?

options.A: Spherical, diameter not less than 30 cm
options.B: Spherical, diameter not less than 60 cm

questions.explanation: Section 6.2.5.4: 'A marker displayed on an overhead wire, cable, etc., should be spherical and have a diameter of not less than 60 cm.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 6, what is required for vehicles and other mobile objects on the movement area?

options.B: They are obstacles and shall be marked and, if used at night or in low visibility, lighted
options.C: Only if they weigh more than 5000 kg

questions.explanation: Section 6.1.1.1: 'Vehicles and other mobile objects, excluding aircraft, on the movement area of an aerodrome are obstacles and shall be marked and, if the vehicles and aerodrome are used at night or in conditions of low visibility, lighted, except that aircraft servicing equipment and vehicles used only on aprons may be exempt.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 6, what is the requirement for marking and lighting of overhead wires, cables, etc., crossing a river, waterway, valley or highway?

options.A: They should be marked and their supporting towers marked and lighted if an aeronautical study indicates they could constitute a hazard
options.B: They must always be marked and lighted regardless of hazard
options.C: They require no marking or lighting
options.D: Only the wires need to be marked
options.E: Only the supporting towers need to be lighted

questions.explanation: Section 6.2.5.1 and 6.2.5.2, along with 6.1.1.10 and 6.1.2.3, indicate that overhead wires, cables, etc., should be marked and their supporting towers marked and lighted if an aeronautical study indicates they could constitute a hazard to aircraft.

questions.question: For a wind turbine with overall height between 150 m and 315 m, what additional obstacle lighting is required beyond the medium-intensity light on the nacelle?

options.A: A second light serving as an alternate, and an intermediate level at half the nacelle height of at least three low-intensity Type E lights
options.B: High-intensity lights at the top only
options.D: Only a second light on the nacelle
options.F: Flashing red lights on the blades

questions.explanation: Section 6.2.4.3 e) ii) and iii): For wind turbines from 150 m to 315 m in overall height, in addition to the medium-intensity light installed on the nacelle, a second light serving as an alternate should be provided, and an intermediate level at half the nacelle height of at least three low-intensity Type E lights should be provided.

questions.question: When a runway or taxiway is permanently closed, what must be done with the markings?

options.A: They must be covered with temporary material
options.C: They must be painted over with yellow
options.D: They must be left as is
options.E: They must be converted to closed markings
options.F: They must be removed by grinding

questions.explanation: Section 7.1.5: 'When a runway or taxiway or portion thereof is permanently closed, all normal runway and taxiway markings shall be obliterated.'

questions.question: What is the pattern of a closed marking on a runway?

options.B: A white X with arms at 45° to the centre line (Figure 7-1 illustration a)
options.D: A solid white line across the runway

questions.explanation: Section 7.1.4: 'The closed marking shall be of the form and proportions as detailed in Figure 7-1, illustration a), when displayed on a runway...' The figure shows a white X with arms at 45° to the centre line.
questions.image_description: Figure 7-1 illustration a) shows a white X marking for closed runway

questions.question: What is the recommended colour for a pre-threshold chevron marking?
questions.explanation: Section 7.3.3: 'A chevron marking should be of conspicuous colour and contrast with the colour used for the runway markings; it should preferably be yellow.'

questions.question: What colour are unserviceability lights?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.4: 'An unserviceability light shall consist of a red fixed light.'

questions.question: What is the required minimum intensity of unserviceability lights?
questions.explanation: Section 7.4.4: 'In no case shall the intensity be less than 10 cd of red light.'

questions.question: For a precision approach runway category I, what is the maximum switch-over time required for the approach lighting system?
questions.explanation: Table 8-1: For precision approach category I, the approach lighting system maximum switch-over time is 15 seconds.

questions.question: For a precision approach runway category II/III, what is the maximum switch-over time for the inner 300 m of the approach lighting system?
questions.explanation: Table 8-1: For precision approach category II/III, inner 300 m of the approach lighting system maximum switch-over time is 1 second.

questions.question: For a runway meant for take-off in runway visual range conditions less than 800 m, what is the maximum switch-over time for runway centre line lights?
questions.explanation: Table 8-1: For runway meant for take-off in RVR < 800 m, runway centre line lights maximum switch-over time is 1 second.

questions.question: For a stop bar at a runway-holding position, what is the maximum time for an indication of a change in operational status to be provided (recommended)?
questions.explanation: Section 8.3.3: 'Where a change in the operational status of lights has occurred, an indication should be provided within two seconds for a stop bar at a runway-holding position and within five seconds for all other types of visual aids.'

questions.question: How often must a full-scale aerodrome emergency exercise be conducted?

options.B: At intervals not exceeding two years
options.C: At intervals not exceeding three years

questions.explanation: Section 9.1.13 a): 'a full-scale aerodrome emergency exercise at intervals not exceeding two years and partial emergency exercises in the intervening year...'

questions.question: What is the operational objective for rescue and firefighting response time to any point of each operational runway in optimum visibility and surface conditions?
questions.explanation: Section 9.2.26: 'The operational objective of the rescue and firefighting service shall be to achieve a response time not exceeding three minutes to any point of each operational runway, in optimum visibility and surface conditions.'

questions.question: True or False: A fence or other suitable barrier must be provided on an aerodrome only to prevent entrance of animals, not unauthorized persons.
questions.explanation: Section 9.10.1 requires a fence to prevent entrance of animals large enough to be a hazard. Section 9.10.2 requires a fence to deter access of unauthorized persons onto a non-public area. So both purposes.

questions.question: True or False: The aerodrome emergency plan must be tested by a full-scale exercise at intervals not exceeding three years, with no partial exercises required.
questions.explanation: Section 9.1.13: The plan shall be tested by conducting a full-scale exercise at intervals not exceeding two years and partial emergency exercises in the intervening year, or a series of modular tests concluding in a full-scale exercise at intervals not exceeding three years.

questions.question: What is the response time objective for the first responding rescue and firefighting vehicle to any other part of the movement area (optimum conditions)?
questions.explanation: Section 9.2.28: 'The operational objective of the rescue and firefighting service should be to achieve a response time not exceeding three minutes to any other part of the movement area, in optimum visibility and surface conditions.'

questions.question: For an aerodrome category 6, what is the minimum number of rescue and firefighting vehicles recommended?
questions.explanation: Section 9.2.40 table: Aerodrome category 6 requires 3 rescue and firefighting vehicles.

questions.question: What is the principal extinguishing agent recommended for aerodromes in categories 1 to 3?

options.B: Foam meeting performance level B or C (preferably)

questions.explanation: Section 9.2.9: '...except that the principal extinguishing agent for aerodromes in categories 1 to 3 should preferably meet a performance level B or C foam.'

questions.question: According to Annex 14, what must be done to decrease the risk of wildlife strikes?

options.A: All wildlife on the aerodrome must be exterminated
options.B: Action shall be taken to adopt measures to minimize the likelihood of collisions between wildlife and aircraft
options.C: No action is required if strikes are rare
options.D: Only bird strikes need to be reported
options.E: A wildlife management programme is optional
options.F: Only mammals need to be controlled

questions.explanation: Section 9.4.3: 'Action shall be taken to decrease the risk to aircraft operations by adopting measures to minimize the likelihood of collisions between wildlife and aircraft.'

questions.question: True or False: An apron management service shall be provided with radiotelephony communications facilities.
questions.explanation: Section 9.5.3: 'An apron management service shall be provided with radiotelephony communications facilities.'

questions.question: During ground servicing of an aircraft, what fire extinguishing provision must be readily available?

options.B: Fire extinguishing equipment suitable for at least initial intervention in event of fuel fire
options.D: No equipment required if aircraft is small

questions.explanation: Section 9.6.1: 'Fire extinguishing equipment suitable for at least initial intervention in the event of a fuel fire and personnel trained in its use shall be readily available during the ground servicing of an aircraft...'

questions.question: Who shall authorize a vehicle to operate on the manoeuvring area?
questions.explanation: Section 9.7.1 a): 'A vehicle shall be operated: a) on a manoeuvring area only as authorized by the aerodrome control tower;'

questions.question: What is the requirement for equipment or installations that must be located within 75 m of the runway centre line for code number 3 or 4?

options.B: They shall be frangible and mounted as low as possible
options.D: They must be marked with flags
options.E: They must be enclosed by a fence

questions.explanation: Section 9.9.2: 'Any equipment or installation required for air navigation or for aircraft safety purposes which must be located: a) on that portion of a runway strip within: 1) 75 m of the runway centre line where the code number is 3 or 4; ... shall be frangible and mounted as low as possible.'

questions.question: What is the requirement for the driver of a vehicle on the movement area regarding mandatory instructions conveyed by lights?

options.A: They may be ignored if no other traffic is present
options.B: The driver shall comply with all mandatory instructions conveyed by lights
options.C: Only red lights must be obeyed
options.D: Only flashing lights must be obeyed
options.E: Instructions from lights are advisory only
options.F: The driver may proceed if safe

questions.explanation: Section 9.7.3: 'The driver of a vehicle on the movement area shall comply with all mandatory instructions conveyed by lights.'

questions.question: What is the minimum response time objective for rescue and firefighting vehicles required to deliver the amounts of extinguishing agents specified in Table 9-2 (other than the first responding vehicles)?

options.A: No more than 2 minutes from initial call
options.B: No more than 3 minutes from initial call
options.C: No more than 4 minutes from initial call
options.D: No more than 5 minutes from initial call
options.E: No more than 6 minutes from initial call
options.F: No more than 10 minutes from initial call

questions.explanation: Section 9.2.30: 'Any vehicles, other than the first responding vehicle(s), required to deliver the amounts of extinguishing agents specified in Table 9-2 shall ensure continuous agent application and shall arrive no more than four minutes from the initial call.'

questions.question: What is the requirement for protective clothing for rescue and firefighting personnel?

options.B: All responding rescue and firefighting personnel shall be provided with protective clothing and respiratory equipment
options.C: Only the vehicle driver needs protective clothing
options.D: Protective clothing is optional for category 1 and 2 aerodromes

questions.explanation: Section 9.2.45: 'All responding rescue and firefighting personnel shall be provided with protective clothing and respiratory equipment to enable them to perform their duties in an effective manner.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 9, what should a disabled aircraft removal plan be based on?

options.A: The characteristics of aircraft that may normally be expected to operate at the aerodrome
options.B: The largest aircraft in the world
options.C: Only the aircraft that have previously had accidents at the aerodrome

questions.explanation: Section 9.3.2: 'The disabled aircraft removal plan should be based on the characteristics of the aircraft that may normally be expected to operate at the aerodrome, and include among other things: a) a list of equipment and personnel on, or in the vicinity of, the aerodrome which would be available for such purpose; and b) arrangements for the rapid receipt of aircraft recovery equipment kits available from other aerodromes.'

questions.question: What must be provided to an aircraft stand to ensure recommended clearance distances?
questions.explanation: Section 9.5.7: 'An aircraft stand shall be visually monitored to ensure that the recommended clearance distances are provided to an aircraft using the stand.'

questions.question: True or False: During low visibility procedures, persons and vehicles operating on an apron may continue normal operations without restriction.
questions.explanation: Section 9.5.4: 'Where low visibility procedures are in effect, persons and vehicles operating on an apron shall be restricted to the essential minimum.'

questions.question: What is the purpose of a surface movement guidance and control system (SMGCS)?

options.B: To provide guidance and control for surface movement at an aerodrome

questions.explanation: Section 9.8.1: 'A surface movement guidance and control system (SMGCS) shall be provided at an aerodrome.' The purpose is implied from the name and the subsequent design recommendations (9.8.2 to 9.8.5).

questions.question: What is the requirement for a fence at an aerodrome regarding animals?

options.A: A fence shall be provided to prevent entrance of animals large enough to be a hazard to aircraft
options.B: A fence is only required for security purposes
options.C: A fence is optional for animal control
options.E: A fence is only required for large animals like deer
options.F: No fence is required for animals

questions.explanation: Section 9.10.1: 'A fence or other suitable barrier shall be provided on an aerodrome to prevent the entrance to the movement area of animals large enough to be a hazard to aircraft.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 9, what is the role of the emergency operations centre?

options.B: To provide overall coordination and general direction of the response to an emergency
options.C: To manage air traffic during an emergency

questions.explanation: Section 9.1.8: 'The emergency operations centre should be a part of the aerodrome facilities and should be responsible for the overall coordination and general direction of the response to an emergency.'

questions.question: What is the minimum number of rescue and firefighting vehicles for aerodrome category 10?
questions.explanation: Section 9.2.40 table: Aerodrome category 10 requires 3 rescue and firefighting vehicles.

questions.question: True or False: The driver of a radio-equipped vehicle must establish two-way radio communication with the aerodrome control tower before entering the apron only, not the manoeuvring area.
questions.explanation: Section 9.7.5: 'The driver of a radio-equipped vehicle shall establish satisfactory two-way radio communication with the aerodrome control tower before entering the manoeuvring area and with the appropriate designated authority before entering the apron.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 4, what is the slope of the conical surface?
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: Conical slope is 5% for all runway classifications and code numbers.

questions.question: What is the height of the inner horizontal surface for an aerodrome with code number 3 or 4 (precision approach)?
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: For precision approach runways (categories I, II, III) with code numbers 1,2,3,4, the inner horizontal height is 45 m.

questions.question: For a precision approach runway category I, what is the slope of the inner approach surface?
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: For precision approach (categories I, II, III) with code numbers 1,2,3,4, the inner approach slope is 2% (for code numbers 3,4) or 2.5% (for code numbers 1,2). For category I specifically, code numbers 3 and 4 have inner approach slope 2%.

questions.question: For a non-instrument runway with code number 1, what is the length of the inner edge of the approach surface?
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: For non-instrument runways code number 1, length of inner edge is 60 m.

questions.question: What is the slope of the take-off climb surface for code number 3 or 4?
questions.explanation: Table 4-2: For runways meant for take-off, code number 3 or 4, slope is 2%.

questions.question: True or False: For a precision approach runway category II or III, fixed objects are permitted above the inner approach surface only if they are frangible and required for navigation.
questions.explanation: Section 4.2.18: 'Fixed objects shall not be permitted above the inner approach surface, the inner transitional surface or the balked landing surface, except for frangible objects which because of their function must be located on the strip.'

questions.question: What is the recommended height above ground elevation for objects to be regarded as obstacles outside obstacle limitation surfaces?
questions.explanation: Section 4.3.2: 'In areas beyond the limits of the obstacle limitation surfaces, at least those objects which extend to a height of 150 m or more above ground elevation should be regarded as obstacles, unless a special aeronautical study indicates that they do not constitute a hazard to aeroplanes.'

questions.question: For a precision approach runway category II or III, which obstacle limitation surfaces shall be established?

options.A: Only inner approach and inner transitional surfaces
options.B: Conical, inner horizontal, approach and inner approach, transitional, inner transitional, and balked landing surfaces

questions.explanation: Section 4.2.15: 'The following obstacle limitation surfaces shall be established for a precision approach runway category II or III: conical surface; inner horizontal surface; approach surface and inner approach surface; transitional surfaces; inner transitional surfaces; and balked landing surface.'

questions.question: What is the description of the conical surface in Annex 14 Volume I?

options.A: A surface located in a horizontal plane above an aerodrome
options.B: A surface sloping upwards and outwards from the periphery of the inner horizontal surface
options.C: An inclined plane preceding the threshold
options.D: A surface along the side of the strip sloping upwards and outwards
options.E: An inclined plane located after the threshold
options.F: A surface beyond the end of a runway for take-off

questions.explanation: Section 4.1.1: 'Conical surface. A surface sloping upwards and outwards from the periphery of the inner horizontal surface.'

questions.question: What is the slope of the approach surface for a precision approach runway category I with code number 4?
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: For precision approach runway (categories I, II, III) code number 4, the slope of the first section of the approach surface is 2% (second section slope is 2.5% for some categories, but the first section slope for code number 4 is 2%).

questions.question: True or False: The inner horizontal surface height for a non-instrument runway with code number 4 is 100 m according to Table 4-1.
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: For non-instrument runway code number 4, inner horizontal height is 100 m.

questions.question: For a non-precision approach runway, what is the divergence (each side) of the approach surface?
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: For non-precision approach runway (all code numbers), divergence each side is 15%.

questions.question: What is the final width of the take-off climb surface for code number 3 or 4 when the intended track does not include changes of heading greater than 15 degrees?
questions.explanation: Table 4-2: For code number 3 or 4, final width is 1 200 m (or 1 800 m if changes of heading greater than 15° for operations in IMC, VMC by night). The question specifies 'not greater than 15°', so 1 200 m.

questions.question: For a non-precision approach runway, what is the distance from the threshold to the inner edge of the approach surface?
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: For non-precision approach runways, distance from threshold to inner edge is 60 m for all code numbers.

questions.question: What is the slope of the transitional surface for a non-instrument runway with code number 4?
questions.explanation: Table 4-1: For non-instrument runway code number 4, transitional slope is 20% (the table shows 20% for non-instrument code numbers 3 and 4, and 14.3% for code numbers 1 and 2).

questions.question: What is the minimum character height for a mandatory instruction sign on a runway code number 3 or 4?
questions.explanation: Appendix 4, section 1 table: For runway code number 3 or 4, mandatory instruction sign minimum character height is 400 mm.

questions.question: What is the required average luminance for a mandatory instruction sign (red background) when operations are conducted in RVR less than 800 m?
questions.explanation: Appendix 4, section 4 a): Where operations are conducted in runway visual range conditions less than a value of 800 m, average sign luminance shall be at least: Red 30 cd/m², Yellow 150 cd/m², White 300 cd/m².

questions.question: In Appendix 4, what is the minimum character height for an information sign (other than runway exit/vacated) on runway code number 1 or 2?
questions.explanation: Appendix 4, section 1 table: For runway code number 1 or 2, information sign (other signs) minimum character height is 200 mm.

questions.question: True or False: In Appendix 4, the stroke width for a legend height of 400 mm is 64 mm.
questions.explanation: Appendix 4, section 3: Stroke width for legend height 400 mm is 64 mm (table: 200 mm -> 32 mm, 300 mm -> 48 mm, 400 mm -> 64 mm).

questions.question: In Appendix 4, what is the minimum face height for a sign with legend height of 400 mm?
questions.explanation: Appendix 4, section 10: Legend height 400 mm -> Face height (min) 600 mm.

questions.question: According to Appendix 4, what is the minimum character height for a runway exit sign on runway code number 1 or 2?
questions.explanation: Appendix 4, section 1 table: For runway code number 1 or 2, mandatory instruction sign (runway exit sign falls under mandatory instruction) minimum character height is 300 mm. Specifically, 'Runway exit and runway vacated signs' are listed separately? The table shows: Mandatory instruction sign: 300 mm for code 1 or 2; Runway exit and runway vacated signs: 300 mm; Other signs: 200 mm. So runway exit sign is 300 mm.

questions.question: According to Chapter 10, what is the objective of a maintenance programme at an aerodrome?

options.B: To maintain facilities in a condition which does not impair the safety, regularity or efficiency of air navigation
options.C: To extend the life of equipment

questions.explanation: Section 10.1.1: 'A maintenance programme, including preventive maintenance where appropriate, shall be established at an aerodrome to maintain facilities in a condition which does not impair the safety, regularity or efficiency of air navigation.'

questions.question: For a paved runway, how often should surface friction characteristics be measured for maintenance purposes?

options.B: Periodically, with sufficient frequency to determine the trend

questions.explanation: Section 10.2.4: 'Runway surface friction characteristics for maintenance purposes shall be periodically measured with a continuous friction measuring device using self-wetting features and documented. The frequency of these measurements shall be sufficient to determine the trend of the surface friction characteristics of the runway.'

questions.question: What is the maximum allowable longitudinal slope for a temporary ramp during runway pavement overlay, for overlays more than 5 cm in thickness?
questions.explanation: Section 10.4.1: 'The longitudinal slope of the temporary ramp, measured with reference to the existing runway surface or previous overlay course, shall be: a) 0.5 to 1.0 per cent for overlays up to and including 5 cm in thickness; and b) not more than 0.5 per cent for overlays more than 5 cm in thickness.'

questions.question: When is a light deemed unserviceable according to Chapter 10?

options.B: When the main beam average intensity is less than 50% of the value specified in Appendix 2
options.D: When it is out of alignment

questions.explanation: Section 10.5.1: 'A light shall be deemed to be unserviceable when the main beam average intensity is less than 50 per cent of the value specified in the appropriate figure in Appendix 2.'

questions.question: For a precision approach runway category II or III, what is the minimum serviceability objective for lights in the inner 450 m of the approach lighting system?
questions.explanation: Section 10.5.7 a) 1): '95 per cent of the lights are serviceable in each of the following particular significant elements: 1) precision approach category II and III lighting system, the inner 450 m;'

questions.question: For a precision approach runway category I, what is the minimum serviceability objective for the precision approach category I lighting system?
questions.explanation: Section 10.5.10: 'The system of preventive maintenance employed for a precision approach runway category I shall have as its objective that, during any period of category I operations, all approach and runway lights are serviceable and that, in any event, at least 85 per cent of the lights are serviceable in each of the following: a) precision approach category I lighting system; ...'

questions.question: According to Chapter 10, what should be inspected regularly as part of a preventive and corrective maintenance programme?

options.B: The surfaces of all movement areas including pavements (runways, taxiways, aprons) and adjacent areas

questions.explanation: Section 10.2.1: 'The surfaces of all movement areas including pavements (runways, taxiways and aprons) and adjacent areas shall be inspected and their conditions monitored regularly as part of an aerodrome preventive and corrective maintenance programme...'

questions.question: What chemicals shall not be used for pavement de-icing according to Chapter 10?

options.B: Chemicals which may have harmful effects on aircraft or pavements, or toxic effects on the environment

questions.explanation: Section 10.3.6: 'Chemicals which may have harmful effects on aircraft or pavements, or chemicals which may have toxic effects on the aerodrome environment, shall not be used.'

questions.question: For a precision approach runway category II or III, what is the recommended frequency of measurement of lights (in-pavement lights)?
questions.explanation: Section 10.5.6: 'The frequency of measurement of lights for a precision approach runway category II or III should be based on ... but, in any event, should not be less than twice a year for in-pavement lights and not less than once a year for other lights.'

questions.question: True or False: For a precision approach runway category II or III, an unserviceable light may be permitted adjacent to another unserviceable light in a barrette or crossbar.
questions.explanation: Section 10.5.7: '... an unserviceable light shall not be permitted adjacent to another unserviceable light, except in a barrette or a crossbar where two adjacent unserviceable lights may be permitted.'

questions.question: What is the recommended action when the runway surface friction characteristics fall below the minimum friction level specified by the State?

options.B: Corrective maintenance action shall be taken

questions.explanation: Section 10.2.7: 'Corrective maintenance action shall be taken to prevent the runway surface friction characteristics for either the entire runway or a portion thereof from falling below a minimum friction level specified by the State.'

questions.question: What is the recommended action for a temporary ramp during runway pavement overlay regarding the entire width of the runway?

options.B: The entire width of the runway should be overlaid during each work session

questions.explanation: Section 10.4.3: 'The entire width of the runway should be overlaid during each work session.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 10, what is the minimum serviceability objective for runway edge lights on a precision approach runway category II or III?
questions.explanation: Section 10.5.7 a) 4): '95 per cent of the lights are serviceable in each of the following particular significant elements: ... 4) runway edge lights;'

questions.question: For a stop bar provided at a runway-holding position used with a runway intended for operations in RVR less than 350 m, what is the serviceability objective regarding unserviceable lights?

options.A: No more than one light unserviceable
options.B: No more than two lights unserviceable, and two adjacent lights not remaining unserviceable unless spacing is less than specified

questions.explanation: Section 10.5.8: 'The system of preventive maintenance employed for a stop bar ... shall have the following objectives: a) no more than two lights will remain unserviceable; and b) two adjacent lights will not remain unserviceable unless the light spacing is significantly less than that specified.'

questions.question: According to Appendix 1, what is the yellow boundary for red aeronautical ground lights with filament-type sources, except for visual approach slope indicator systems?
questions.explanation: Appendix 1, section 2.1.1 a): Red - Yellow boundary: y = 0.335, except for visual approach slope indicator systems (for which y = 0.320).

questions.question: What is the recommended luminance factor for ordinary colour red for markings and externally illuminated signs and panels?
questions.explanation: Appendix 1, section 3.2 a): Red - Luminance factor ß = 0.07 (min).

questions.question: In Appendix 1, for solid state light sources (LEDs), what is the green boundary for the colour green?
questions.explanation: Appendix 1, section 2.3.1 c): Green - Yellow boundary: x = 0.310.

questions.question: In Appendix 1, for filament-type lights, what is the yellow boundary for green?
questions.explanation: Appendix 1, section 2.1.1 c): Green - Yellow boundary: x = 0.360 - 0.080y.

questions.question: In Appendix 2, Figure A2-1, what is the minimum average intensity for approach centre line light (white light)?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Figure A2-1: Minimum average intensity is 20 000 cd (labelled on the diagram).

questions.question: True or False: According to Appendix 2, the average intensity of a light should never fall to a value less than 50% of the value shown in the figures.
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Collective notes to Figures A2-1 to A2-11 and A2-26, note 8: 'The importance of adequate maintenance cannot be overemphasized. The average intensity should never fall to a value less than 50 per cent of the value shown in the figures...'

questions.question: According to Appendix 2, for a runway centre line light with 30 m longitudinal spacing (white light), what is the minimum average intensity?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Figure A2-6: Minimum average intensity is 5 000 cd (shown on diagram).

questions.question: In Appendix 2, Figure A2-12, what is the minimum intensity for taxiway centre line lights (15 m spacing) for RVR less than 350 m?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Figure A2-12: Minimum intensity is 20 cd (labelled on diagram).

questions.question: In Appendix 2, Figure A2-3 (threshold light), what is the minimum average intensity for green light?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Figure A2-3: Minimum average intensity is 10 000 cd (labelled on the diagram).

questions.question: According to Appendix 5, for a structure with height 150 m, what type of obstacle lighting is recommended on structures with a height of more than 150 m above ground level?
questions.explanation: Appendix 5, note under Figure A5-1: 'High-intensity obstacle lighting is recommended on structures with a height of more than 150 m above ground level.'

questions.question: According to Appendix 5, for a medium-intensity dual obstacle lighting system Type A/Type B, which lights are used when background luminescence is below 50 cd/m² (night)?

options.B: Medium-intensity Type B and low-intensity Type B

questions.explanation: Appendix 5, Figure A5-4 note: Medium-intensity obstacle light Type B < 50 cd/m², Low-intensity obstacle light Type B < 50 cd/m² are used at night.

questions.question: In Appendix 5, for a medium-intensity flashing-white obstacle lighting system (Type A) on a structure of height 150 m, how many levels of lights are shown?
questions.explanation: Appendix 5, Figure A5-1: For a structure height of 150 m, lights are placed at top, at 105 m, and at 45 m? Actually the figure shows levels: top, at 105 m, and at 45 m? For 150 m, the diagram indicates 3 levels: 0? Let's see: The figure has a scale. Typically, for 150 m, there are lights at the top, at 105 m, and at 45 m (or ground). But the recommended spacing is 105 m maximum. For 150 m, you would have top, then one intermediate at 105 m, and ground? That's 3 levels. The correct answer is 3.

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 1.1.3, which factor is NOT listed under 'Topography of the aerodrome site'?

options.E: Possibility of installing visual and non-visual aids

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 1.1.3 lists a) compliance with OLS, b) land use, c) runway lengths, d) construction costs, e) possibility of installing aids. Proximity of other aerodromes is under 1.1.4 (Air traffic in vicinity).

questions.question: What is the recommended maximum spacing between markers on overhead wires, for a marker diameter of 60 cm?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 6.2.5.5 a): '30 m where the marker diameter is 60 cm'

questions.question: In the context of runway surface evenness (Attachment A), what is the acceptable surface irregularity height for a length of 3 m?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 5.3 table: For length of irregularity 3 m, acceptable surface irregularity height is 2.9 cm.

questions.question: According to Attachment A, what is the mean texture depth classification for ESDU class E?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 7.3.9 table: Classification E texture depths: 1.01 – 2.54 mm.

questions.question: In Attachment A, what is the recommended maximum spacing between unserviceability area lights per 7.5 m of peripheral distance?

options.A: At least one light for each 5 m
options.B: At least one light for each 7.5 m
options.C: At least one light for each 10 m
options.D: At least one light for each 15 m
options.E: At least one light for each 20 m
options.F: At least one light for each 30 m

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 13: 'At least one light should be installed for each 7.5 m of peripheral distance of the area.'

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 20 (ARIWS), what should flight crews do when they see red lights from an autonomous runway incursion warning system?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 20.2.1: 'Pilots are presented with a globally consistent signal which means 'STOP IMMEDIATELY' and must be taught to react accordingly. Likewise, pilots receiving an ATS clearance to take-off or cross a runway, and seeing the red light array, must STOP and advise ATS that they aborted/stopped because of the red lights.'

questions.topic: Taxiway design for runway incursion prevention
questions.question: In Attachment A, section 21 (taxiway design to minimize runway incursions), what is recommended regarding the centre line of an entrance taxiway relative to the runway centre line?

options.A: It should be parallel to the runway centre line
options.B: It should be perpendicular to the runway centre line, where possible
options.F: It should be offset by 10 m

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 21.2: 'The centre line of an entrance taxiway should be perpendicular to the runway centre line, where possible.'

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 2 (clearways and stopways), what is the relationship between the decision speed and the accelerate-stop distance available?

options.A: A lower decision speed requires a longer accelerate-stop distance available
options.B: A higher decision speed is selected as the accelerate-stop distance available increases
options.C: Decision speed is fixed regardless of accelerate-stop distance
options.D: Decision speed decreases as accelerate-stop distance increases
options.F: Decision speed is determined by wind speed

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 2.4: 'Normally, a higher decision speed is selected as the accelerate-stop distance available increases.'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 4.3 (radio altimeter operating area), what is the recommended width of the area when special circumstances warrant a reduction?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 4.3: 'When special circumstances so warrant, the width may be reduced to no less than 60 m if an aeronautical study indicates that such reduction would not affect the safety of operations of aircraft.' Wait, the text says: 'The area should be symmetrical about the extended centre line, 120 m wide. When special circumstances so warrant, the width may be reduced to no less than 60 m...' Actually re-reading: 'This area should be symmetrical about the extended centre line, 120 m wide. When special circumstances so warrant, the width may be reduced to no less than 60 m...' So the answer is 60 m, not 30. Correction: The reduced width is not less than 60 m. Options include 60 m as B. Let me adjust the correct answer accordingly.

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 5 (runway surface evenness), what is the maximum acceptable height for a step type bump at zero length?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 5.3: 'The maximum tolerable step type bump ... is simply the bump height corresponding to zero bump length at the upper end of the tolerable region of the roughness criteria of Figure A-3. The bump height at this location is 1.75 cm.'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 6 (runway condition report), what is the purpose of the runway condition code (RWYCC)?

options.A: To classify the airport's maintenance level
options.B: To permit an operational aeroplane performance calculation by the flight crew
options.D: To set speed limits on taxiways

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 6.2 b): 'a unique runway condition code (RWYCC) linking the agreed set of criteria with the aircraft landing and take-off performance table, and related to the braking action experienced and eventually reported by flight crews;' Also section 6.3 refers to correlation with expected braking action.

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 7 (drainage), what is the threshold value between microtexture and macrotexture using the volumetric method?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 7.3.11: 'These standards describe the threshold value between microtexture and macrotexture as 0.5 mm.'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 8 (strips), what is the recommended graded width for a precision approach runway with code number 3 or 4?

options.B: 105 m from centre line, reducing to 75 m at both ends

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 8.3: 'Figure A-4 shows the shape and dimensions of a wider strip that may be considered for such a runway. The portion to be graded extends to a distance of 105 m from the centre line, except that the distance is gradually reduced to 75 m from the centre line at both ends of the strip, for a length of 150 m from the runway end.'
questions.image_description: Figure A-4

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 9 (runway end safety areas), what is the recommended length of RESA for a precision approach runway where the ILS localizer is the first upstanding obstacle?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 9.1: 'On a precision approach runway, the ILS localizer is normally the first upstanding obstacle, and the runway end safety area should extend up to this facility.'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 10 (location of threshold), what is the recommended maximum slope of the obstacle free surface to the threshold for code number 4?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 10.2.4: 'the selected position for the threshold should not be such that the obstacle free surface to the threshold is steeper than 3.3 per cent where the code number is 4 or steeper than 5 per cent where the code number is 3.'

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 11 (approach lighting systems), what is the maximum tolerance for the centre line of an approach lighting system relative to the extended runway centre line?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 11.2.2: 'The centre line of an approach lighting system should be as coincident as possible with the extended centre line of the runway with a maximum tolerance of ±15'.'

questions.question: True or False: In Attachment A, section 17 (rescue and firefighting), the training curriculum for rescue personnel must include instruction in dangerous goods.
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 17.2 lists 'j) dangerous goods' as part of the training curriculum.

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 19 (overload operations using ACN-PCN method applicable until 27 Nov 2024), for flexible pavements, occasional movements by aircraft with ACN not exceeding what percentage above the reported PCN should not adversely affect the pavement?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 19.1.1 a): 'for flexible pavements, occasional movements by aircraft with ACN not exceeding 10 per cent above the reported PCN should not adversely affect the pavement;'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 21 (taxiway design for runway incursion prevention), what is recommended regarding multi-taxiway entrances to a runway?

options.A: They should converge into one wide entrance
options.B: They should be parallel to each other and distinctly separated by an unpaved area
options.C: They should be perpendicular to the runway
options.D: They should be offset by 30 degrees
options.E: They should share a common holding position

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 21.5: 'Multi-taxiway entrances to a runway should be parallel to each other and should be distinctly separated by an unpaved area.'

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 22 (aerodrome mapping data), what are the two levels of quality for aerodrome mapping databases?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 22, reference to Note 2 in 2.1.3: 'Aerodrome mapping databases can be provided at one of two levels of quality — fine or medium.'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 1.1.2, what factor is NOT listed as requiring reduction of maximum crosswind component?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 1.1.2 b) lists factors: handling characteristics, prevalence and nature of gusts, prevalence and nature of turbulence, availability of a secondary runway, width of runways, runway surface conditions, strength of wind. Aircraft colour is not mentioned.

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 3, when a runway has a displaced threshold, which declared distance is affected?

options.B: LDA for approaches made to that threshold

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 3.5: 'If a runway has a displaced threshold, then the LDA will be reduced by the distance the threshold is displaced, as shown in Figure A-1 (D). A displaced threshold affects only the LDA for approaches made to that threshold; all declared distances for operations in the reciprocal direction are unaffected.'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 5.3, what is the acceptable surface irregularity height for a length of 45 m?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 5.3 table: For length 45 m, acceptable surface irregularity height is 8.5 cm.

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 7.3.9, what is the texture depth for ESDU classification C?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 7.3.9 table: Classification C texture depths: 0.25 – 0.50 mm.

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 11.3.4, for an ILS localizer located 300 m from the threshold, what is the maximum height it may extend above the plane of the approach lighting system?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 11.3.4: 'the screen will be permitted to extend above the plane of the approach lighting system by 10 × 15 = 150 cm maximum' (since 300/30 = 10, times 15 cm = 150 cm).

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 14 (RETILs), what is the recommended roll-out speed until the first RETIL (three-light barrette) is reached?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 14.3: 'A roll-out speed of 60 knots until the first RETIL (three-light barrette) is reached is seen as the optimum.'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 18 (operators of vehicles), what knowledge should vehicle operators have as appropriate to their function?

options.A: Aerodrome geography, signs, markings, lights, radiotelephone procedures, ATC terms, airport rules, and specialist functions

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 18.1 lists: geography, aerodrome signs, markings and lights, radiotelephone operating procedures, terms and phrases used in aerodrome control including ICAO spelling alphabet, rules of air traffic services, airport rules and procedures, and specialist functions as required.

questions.question: According to Attachment A, section 19.1.1 (applicable as of 28 November 2024 for ACR-PCR), what is the allowable overload percentage for flexible and rigid pavements?
questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 19.1.1 a) (applicable as of 28 November 2024): 'for flexible and rigid pavements, occasional movements by aircraft with ACR not exceeding 10 per cent above the reported PCR should not adversely affect the pavement;'

questions.question: In Attachment A, section 20 (ARIWS), in case the system fails in the illuminated condition, what is recommended?

options.B: Procedures should be developed by ATS and/or aerodrome operator
options.D: The system must be repaired immediately on site

questions.explanation: Attachment A, section 20.2.4: 'Procedures should be developed to address the circumstance where the system fails in the illuminated condition. It will be up to the ATS and/or aerodrome operator to establish those procedures depending on their own circumstances.'

questions.question: According to Attachment B (Figure B-1), what is the slope of the conical surface?
questions.explanation: Attachment B, Figure B-1 (Obstacle limitation surfaces) labels the conical surface with 'Slope 5%'.
questions.image_description: Figure B-1 shows obstacle limitation surfaces with conical slope 5%

questions.question: In Attachment B, Figure B-1, what is the slope of the take-off climb surface for the non-instrument runway shown?
questions.explanation: Attachment B, Figure B-1 labels the take-off climb surface for the non-instrument runway with 'Slope 2%'.
questions.image_description: Figure B-1 shows obstacle limitation surfaces with slopes indicated

questions.question: In Appendix 3, mandatory instruction markings on pavements are formed as if shadowed from characters of an equivalent elevated sign by what factor?
questions.explanation: Appendix 3, Note 3: 'The mandatory instruction markings and information markings on pavements are formed as if shadowed (i.e., stretched) from the characters of an equivalent elevated sign by a factor of 2.5 as shown in Figure A3-1.'

questions.question: What is the primary object of the aeronautical information service (AIS) as stated in ICAO Annex 15, Chapter 1?

options.A: To provide meteorological information to aircraft
options.B: To ensure the flow of aeronautical data and aeronautical information necessary for global air traffic management system safety, regularity, economy and efficiency in an environmentally sustainable manner
options.C: To manage air traffic control towers at international airports
options.D: To issue NOTAMs only for military operations
options.E: To collect charges for aeronautical information products

questions.explanation: Chapter 1, Note 1 states: 'The object of the aeronautical information service (AIS) is to ensure the flow of aeronautical data and aeronautical information necessary for global air traffic management (ATM) system safety, regularity, economy and efficiency in an environmentally sustainable manner.'

questions.question: According to Chapter 1, what can be the consequence of corrupt, erroneous, late or missing aeronautical data and information?

options.A: Only minor delays in flight schedules
options.B: Increased fuel consumption but no safety impact
options.C: Potential to affect the safety of air navigation
options.F: Improved communication between pilots and controllers

questions.explanation: Chapter 1, Note 1 states: 'Corrupt, erroneous, late or missing aeronautical data and aeronautical information can potentially affect the safety of air navigation.'

questions.question: True or False: The Standards and Recommended Practices in Annex 15 are to be used in conjunction with the PANS-ABC (Doc 8400) and PANS-AIM (Doc 10066).
questions.explanation: Chapter 1, Notes 2 and 3 state: 'These Standards and Recommended Practices are to be used in conjunction with the Procedures for Air Navigation Services — ICAO Abbreviations and Codes (PANS-ABC, Doc 8400).' and '...with the Procedures for Air Navigation Services — Aeronautical Information Management (PANS-AIM, Doc 10066).'

questions.question: According to the definition in Chapter 1.1, what is an 'Aerodrome'?

options.A: Only a land area with a control tower
options.B: A defined area on land or water intended to be used wholly or in part for arrival, departure and surface movement of aircraft, including buildings, installations and equipment
options.C: Any field where helicopters can land
options.F: A water area exclusively for seaplanes

questions.explanation: Chapter 1.1 defines: 'Aerodrome. A defined area on land or water (including any buildings, installations and equipment) intended to be used either wholly or in part for the arrival, departure and surface movement of aircraft.'

questions.question: What is 'Aerodrome mapping data (AMD)' collected for?

options.A: Only for aircraft takeoff performance calculations
