questions.explanation: The document describes PSR identification procedures: 'by correlating a particular radar position indication with an aircraft reporting its position over, or as bearing and distance from, a point shown on the situation display, and by ascertaining that the track of the particular radar position is consistent with the aircraft path or reported heading' (Chapter 8, 8.6.2.4.1 a)). The appropriate ATS authority may prescribe additional conditions such as maximum distance from the radar site or above certain levels.

questions.question: What is the required action when the SSR Mode A code shown on the situation display is different from what has been assigned?

options.A: Immediately request the pilot to stop transponder operation
options.B: Request the pilot to confirm the code selected and, if warranted, to reselect the correct code
options.D: Change the assigned code in the flight plan

questions.explanation: The document states: 'When it is observed that the Mode A code shown on the situation display is different to what has been assigned to the aircraft, the pilot shall be requested to confirm the code selected and, if the situation warrants (e.g. not being a case of unlawful interference), to reselect the correct code.' (Chapter 8, 8.5.3.1)

questions.question: What is the criterion for determining that an aircraft has reached a level for the purpose of level occupancy using pressure-altitude information?

options.A: When the altimeter reading matches the assigned level exactly
options.B: When the elapsed time of three display updates, three sensor updates or 15 seconds, whichever is greater, has passed since the pressure-altitude-derived level information indicates it is within the appropriate tolerance of the assigned level
options.C: When the pilot reports reaching the level
options.D: When the aircraft passes the level by 200 ft
options.E: When the controller observes the level change on display
options.F: When the transponder indicates a change

questions.explanation: The document states: 'An aircraft is considered to have reached the level to which it has been cleared when the elapsed time of three display updates, three sensor updates or 15 seconds, whichever is the greater, has passed since the pressure-altitude-derived level information has indicated that it is within the appropriate tolerances of the assigned level, as specified in 8.5.5.2.1.' (Chapter 8, 8.5.5.2.5)

questions.question: What is the minimum vectoring altitude consideration when vectoring an IFR flight off an ATS route?

options.A: The controller must ensure the prescribed obstacle clearance will exist at all times until the pilot resumes own navigation
options.C: The pilot is solely responsible for obstacle clearance
options.D: The aircraft must be vectored only above FL 290
options.E: Vectoring is not permitted off ATS routes
options.F: The controller must coordinate with the pilot's company

questions.explanation: The document states: 'When vectoring an IFR flight and when giving an IFR flight a direct routing which takes the aircraft off an ATS route, the controller shall issue clearances such that the prescribed obstacle clearance will exist at all times until the aircraft reaches the point where the pilot will resume own navigation. When necessary, the relevant minimum vectoring altitude shall include a correction for low temperature effect.' (Chapter 8, 8.6.5.2)

questions.question: What action should a controller take when an unidentified controlled flight enters airspace where radar separation minima are applied?

options.B: Continue providing radar service to identified aircraft provided reasonable assurance exists that the unidentified flight will be identified using SSR/ADS-B/MLAT or gives adequate primary radar return, and separation is maintained between identified flights and any other observed position indications until identified or procedural separation is established
options.C: Stop all radar services until the unidentified flight is identified
options.D: Transfer control to the adjacent unit
options.E: Instruct all aircraft to squawk standby

questions.explanation: The document states: 'In the event that the controller has been notified of a controlled flight entering or about to enter the airspace within which the separation minima specified in 8.7.3 is applied, but has not identified the aircraft, the controller may, if so prescribed by the appropriate ATS authority, continue to provide an ATS surveillance service to identified aircraft provided that: a) reasonable assurance exists that the unidentified controlled flight will be identified using SSR and/or ADS-B and/or MLAT or the flight is being operated by an aircraft of a type which may be expected to give an adequate return on primary radar in the airspace within which the separation is applied; and b) the separation is maintained between identified flights and any other observed ATS surveillance system position indications until either the unidentified controlled flight has been identified or procedural separation has been established.' (Chapter 8, 8.7.2.8)

questions.question: What is the minimum horizontal separation for radar when reduced to 2.5 NM, under what condition?

options.A: Between succeeding aircraft established on the same final approach track within 10 NM of the runway threshold, with conditions on runway occupancy time, braking action, surveillance system update rate, and controller observation
options.B: Between any aircraft at any time if radar accuracy permits

questions.explanation: The document states: 'b) 4.6 km (2.5 NM) between succeeding aircraft which are established on the same final approach track within 18.5 km (10 NM) of the runway threshold. A reduced separation minimum of 4.6 km (2.5 NM) may be applied, provided: i) the average runway occupancy time of landing aircraft is proven ... not to exceed 50 seconds; ii) braking action is reported as good ...; iii) an ATS surveillance system with appropriate azimuth and range resolution and an update rate of 5 seconds or less is used ...; iv) the aerodrome controller is able to observe ... the runway-in-use and associated exit and entry taxiways; v) distance-based wake turbulence separation minima ... do not apply; vi) aircraft approach speeds are closely monitored; vii) aircraft operators and pilots have been made fully aware of the need to exit the runway in an expeditious manner; and viii) procedures concerning the application of the reduced minimum are published in AIPs.' (Chapter 8, 8.7.3.2 b))

questions.question: What is the distance-based wake turbulence separation minimum between a GROUP A preceding aircraft and a GROUP G succeeding aircraft?
questions.explanation: Table 8.7.3.5 shows for preceding aircraft group A and succeeding aircraft group G: 14.9 km (8.0 NM).

questions.question: What is the minimum temperature correction required for minimum vectoring altitude?

options.A: No correction specified, but low temperature effect shall be corrected when necessary

questions.explanation: Chapter 8, 8.6.5.2: 'When necessary, the relevant minimum vectoring altitude shall include a correction for low temperature effect.' No specific value is mandated; it is left to the appropriate authority.

questions.question: True or False: 'ADS-C should be used to vector an aircraft if the surveillance and communications performance are comparable to radar.'
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Information derived from the display of ADS-C information shall not be used to vector an aircraft.' And a note adds: 'Note.— Vectoring using ADS-C may be possible in the future in cases where the surveillance and communications performance are comparable to the performance of radar systems and direct voice communications using VHF.' That is future possibility, not current permission.

questions.question: What is required for initial ADS-C agreements in airspace where procedural separation is applied?

options.B: A periodic contract and an event contract specifying waypoint change, lateral deviation, and level range deviation events

questions.explanation: The document states: 'In airspace where procedural separation is being applied, ADS-C agreements shall, as a minimum, contain the following ADS contracts: a) a periodic contract at an interval appropriate to the airspace requirements; b) an event contract, specifying the following: 1) a waypoint change event; 2) a lateral deviation event; and 3) a level range deviation event.'

questions.question: What is the tolerance value for ADS-C level information in RVSM airspace?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'The tolerance value which shall be used to determine that the ADS-C level information displayed to the controller is accurate shall be ± 60 m (± 200 ft) in RVSM airspace.'

questions.question: When shall an aircraft be considered to have vacated a level for ADS-C purposes?

options.A: When the ADS-C level information indicates a change of more than 90 m (300 ft) in the anticipated direction
options.B: When the pilot reports leaving the level
options.C: When the controller observes a change on the display
options.D: When the aircraft passes the level by 500 ft
options.F: When the aircraft reports reaching the new level

questions.explanation: The document states: 'An aircraft cleared to leave a level is considered to have commenced its manoeuvre and vacated the previously occupied level when the ADS level information indicates a change of more than 90 m (300 ft) in the anticipated direction from its previously assigned level, or verification has been made by receipt of a CPDLC or voice report from the pilot.' So the primary is the 90 m change.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of ATC unit ADS-C ground systems an aircraft must be capable of supporting simultaneously?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'The aircraft must be capable of supporting ADS-C agreements with at least four ATC unit ADS-C ground systems simultaneously.'

questions.question: What is the maximum number of simultaneous ADS-C agreements an aircraft must support according to Chapter 13?
questions.explanation: Chapter 13, 13.4.3.4.1.5: 'The aircraft must be capable of supporting ADS-C agreements with at least four ATC unit ADS-C ground systems simultaneously.'

questions.question: What is the minimum level change for considering that an aircraft has vacated a level for ADS-C purposes?
questions.explanation: Chapter 13, 13.5.2.3: 'An aircraft cleared to leave a level is considered to have commenced its manoeuvre and vacated the previously occupied level when the ADS level information indicates a change of more than 90 m (300 ft) in the anticipated direction from its previously assigned level, or verification has been made by receipt of a CPDLC or voice report from the pilot.'

questions.question: When an ADS-C ground system attempts to establish an agreement but the aircraft cannot support another contract, what information should the aircraft provide?

options.B: The ICAO location indicators of the ground systems with which it currently has contracts

questions.explanation: Chapter 13, 13.4.3.4.1.5.1: 'When an ADS-C ground system attempts to establish an ADS-C agreement with an aircraft and is unable to do so due to the aircraft's inability to support an additional ADS contract, the aircraft should reply with the ICAO location indicators or eight-letter facility indicators of the ground systems with which it currently has contracts, in order for the ATC unit to negotiate a contract release.'

questions.question: True or False: 'CPDLC messages may contain free text elements, but the use of free text should be avoided to reduce misinterpretation.'
questions.explanation: The document states: 'The use of free text message elements by controllers or pilots should be avoided. Note.— While it is recognized that non-routine and emergency situations may necessitate use of free text ... the avoidance of utilizing free text messages is intended to reduce the possibility of misinterpretation and ambiguity.'

questions.question: What message attribute requires a WILCO, UNABLE, or STANDBY response in CPDLC?
questions.explanation: The document Table 14-2 shows response type W/U requires WILCO, UNABLE, STANDBY, etc.

questions.question: What is the phraseology to correct a CPDLC message using voice?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'When voice communications are used to correct a CPDLC message for which no operational response has yet been received, the controller's or pilot's transmission shall be prefaced by the phrase: 'DISREGARD CPDLC (message type) MESSAGE, BREAK' — followed by the correct clearance, instruction, information or request.'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct an aircraft to resume normal CPDLC operations after a stoppage?
questions.explanation: Chapter 14, 14.3.9.2: 'The resumption of the normal use of CPDLC shall be advised using the following phrase: ((call sign) or ALL STATIONS) RESUME NORMAL CPDLC OPERATIONS'

questions.question: What action should a controller take when a CPDLC emergency message is received?

options.B: Acknowledge receipt by the most efficient means available
options.E: Request the pilot to confirm by voice
options.F: Transfer the communication to another sector

questions.explanation: Chapter 14, 14.3.5.1: 'When a CPDLC emergency message is received, the controller shall acknowledge receipt of the message by the most efficient means available.'

questions.question: What information should be included in a NOTAM for a planned shutdown of CPDLC?

options.A: The shutdown period and, if necessary, the voice communication frequencies to be used
options.D: The aircraft that will be affected

questions.explanation: Chapter 14, 14.3.7.1: 'When a system shutdown of the communications network or the CPDLC ground system is planned, a NOTAM shall be published to inform all affected parties of the shutdown period and, if necessary, the details of the voice communication frequencies to be used.'

questions.question: True or False: 'When a pilot reports minimum fuel, it is considered an emergency situation and ATC must give priority.'
questions.explanation: The document states: 'The declaration of MINIMUM FUEL informs ATC that all planned aerodrome options have been reduced to a specific aerodrome of intended landing ... This is not an emergency situation but an indication that an emergency situation is possible should any additional delay occur.'

questions.question: True or False: 'If an aircraft deviates from its ATC clearance due to an ACAS resolution advisory (RA), the controller continues to be responsible for separation until the pilot reports clear of conflict.'
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Once an aircraft departs from its ATC clearance or instruction in compliance with an RA, or a pilot reports an RA, the controller ceases to be responsible for providing separation between that aircraft and any other aircraft affected as a direct consequence of the manoeuvre induced by the RA. The controller shall resume responsibility for providing separation for all the affected aircraft when: a) the controller acknowledges a report from the flight crew that the aircraft has resumed the current clearance; or b) the controller acknowledges a report from the flight crew that the aircraft is resuming the current clearance and issues an alternative clearance which is acknowledged by the flight crew.'

questions.question: What is the required vertical separation between an aircraft dumping fuel and another aircraft behind it within 15 minutes flying time?

options.A: 300 m (1,000 ft) if above, 900 m (3,000 ft) if below
options.B: 500 ft if above, 1000 ft if below
options.C: 1000 ft if above, 2000 ft if below
options.D: 2000 ft if above, 4000 ft if below
options.E: 1500 ft if above, 2500 ft if below

questions.explanation: The document states: 'Other known traffic should be separated from the aircraft dumping fuel by: ... b) vertical separation if behind the aircraft dumping fuel within 15 minutes flying time or a distance of 93 km (50 NM) by: 1) at least 300 m (1,000 ft) if above the aircraft dumping fuel; and 2) at least 900 m (3,000 ft) if below the aircraft dumping fuel.'

questions.question: What is the SSR code for unlawful interference?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Codes 7700, 7600 and 7500 shall be reserved internationally for use by pilots encountering a state of emergency, radiocommunication failure or unlawful interference, respectively.' So 7500 for unlawful interference.

questions.question: According to the document, what action should ATS units take when unlawful interference with an aircraft is suspected?

options.A: Immediately request the aircraft to land at the nearest aerodrome
options.B: Attempt to verify any suspicion by setting the SSR decoder to Mode A Code 7500 and thereafter to Code 7700
options.C: Ignore the situation until confirmed by the pilot
options.D: Instruct the aircraft to change frequency immediately
options.E: Advise all other aircraft to leave the area
options.F: Transmit a general emergency message on 121.5 MHz

questions.explanation: The document states: 'Whenever unlawful interference with an aircraft is suspected, and where automatic distinct display of SSR Mode A Code 7500 and Code 7700 is not provided, the controller shall attempt to verify any suspicion by setting the SSR decoder to Mode A Code 7500 and thereafter to Code 7700.'

questions.question: What action should a pilot take if an emergency descent is required?

options.B: Navigate as deemed appropriate, advise ATC, set transponder to 7700, turn on exterior lights, watch for traffic, and coordinate after descent
options.D: Descend to the nearest safe altitude and then contact ATC

questions.explanation: The document lists the pilot's steps: 'a) navigate as deemed appropriate by the pilot; b) advise the appropriate ATS unit of the emergency descent and, if able, intentions; c) set transponder to Code 7700 and, if applicable, select the appropriate emergency mode on ADS-B and/or ADS-C; d) turn on aircraft exterior lights; e) watch for conflicting traffic; f) when emergency descent is complete, coordinate further intentions with the appropriate ATS unit.'

questions.question: In the event of a complete failure of ground radio equipment used for ATC, what should the controller do?

options.A: Continue normal operations using hand signals
options.B: Attempt to establish communications on 121.5 MHz, inform adjacent units, appraise them of traffic, request assistance, and instruct adjacent units to hold or re-route flights outside the affected area
options.C: Declare an emergency and evacuate the control tower
options.E: Instruct all aircraft to land immediately
options.F: Ignore the failure and hope it resolves

questions.explanation: The document states: 'In the event of complete failure of the ground radio equipment used for ATC, the controller shall: a) ... attempt to establish radiocommunications on that frequency; b) without delay inform all adjacent control positions or ATC units ... of the failure; c) appraise such positions or units of the current traffic situation; d) if practicable, request their assistance ...; and e) instruct adjacent control positions or ATC units to hold or re-route all controlled flights outside the area of responsibility ... until normal services can be resumed.'

questions.question: What is the recommended escape manoeuvre for an aircraft that has encountered a volcanic ash cloud?

options.C: Reverse course and begin a descent if terrain permits
options.D: Increase speed to outrun the ash
options.F: Declare Mayday and maintain level flight

questions.explanation: The document notes: 'Note 1.— Experience has shown that the recommended escape manoeuvre for an aircraft which has encountered an ash cloud is to reverse its course and begin a descent if terrain permits.'

questions.question: True or False: 'When a pilot reports minimum fuel, ATC must immediately declare an emergency and give priority.'
questions.explanation: The document states: 'The declaration of MINIMUM FUEL ... is not an emergency situation but an indication that an emergency situation is possible should any additional delay occur.' (Chapter 15, 15.5.4.1 Note)

questions.question: What action should a pilot take when receiving an emergency descent broadcast unless specifically instructed otherwise?

options.B: Continue according to current clearance and maintain listening watch
options.C: Proceed to the nearest alternate aerodrome

questions.explanation: The document states: 'Unless specifically instructed by the ATS unit to clear the area or threatened by immediate danger, the pilot shall take the following actions: a) continue according to current clearance and maintain listening watch on the frequency in use for any further instructions from the ATS unit; and b) watch for conflicting traffic both visually and by reference to ACAS (if equipped).'

questions.question: What is the minimum vertical separation for fuel dumping below the aircraft dumping fuel?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Other known traffic should be separated from the aircraft dumping fuel by: ... b) vertical separation if behind the aircraft dumping fuel within 15 minutes flying time or a distance of 93 km (50 NM) by: 1) at least 300 m (1,000 ft) if above the aircraft dumping fuel; and 2) at least 900 m (3,000 ft) if below the aircraft dumping fuel.'

questions.question: What is the required angle of turn for an aircraft deviating from cleared track without ATC clearance in oceanic airspace?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'leave the cleared track or ATS route by initially turning at least 30 degrees to the right or to the left, in order to establish and maintain a parallel, same direction track or ATS route offset 5.0 NM (9.3 km).'

questions.question: What is the maximum time after take-off that an aircraft is considered to have vacated a level for procedural separation purposes?
questions.explanation: The document states for communication failure procedures: 'in airspace where procedural separation is being applied, maintain the last assigned speed and level, or minimum flight altitude if higher, for a period of 20 minutes following the aircraft's failure to report its position over a compulsory reporting point and thereafter adjust level and speed in accordance with the filed flight plan.' This 20-minute period is also referenced in other contexts for separation assumptions.

questions.question: What is the minimum vertical separation for emergency separation when the normal VSM is 300 m (1000 ft)?
questions.explanation: Chapter 15, 15.7.1.1: 'If, during an emergency situation, it is not possible to ensure that the applicable horizontal separation can be maintained, emergency separation of half the applicable vertical separation minimum may be used, i.e. 150 m (500 ft) between aircraft in airspace where a vertical separation minimum of 300 m (1 000 ft) is applied.'

questions.question: What is the minimum altitude for fuel dumping, if possible?
questions.explanation: Chapter 15, 15.5.3.1.2 b): 'the level to be used, which should be not less than 1 800 m (6 000 ft).'

questions.question: What is the definition of 'strayed aircraft'?

options.A: An aircraft which has deviated significantly from its intended track or which reports that it is lost
options.B: An aircraft that has been intercepted
options.C: An aircraft that has declared an emergency
options.D: An aircraft that has experienced communication failure
options.E: An aircraft that is not identified on radar
options.F: An aircraft that has been unlawfully interfered with

questions.explanation: The document states: 'Strayed aircraft. An aircraft which has deviated significantly from its intended track or which reports that it is lost.' (Chapter 15, 15.5.1 Note 1)

questions.question: What is the primary objective of the minimum safe altitude warning (MSAW) function?

options.B: To assist in the prevention of controlled flight into terrain accidents
options.D: To alert controllers of unauthorized runway occupancy

questions.explanation: The document states: 'The objective of the MSAW function is to assist in the prevention of controlled flight into terrain accidents by generating, in a timely manner, a warning of the possible infringement of a minimum safe altitude.' (Chapter 15, 15.7.4 Note 1)

questions.question: What action should a controller take when an aircraft reports an ACAS resolution advisory (RA) that requires deviation from clearance?

options.A: Immediately issue new instructions to resolve the conflict
options.B: Not attempt to modify the aircraft flight path until the pilot reports 'Clear of Conflict'
options.C: Ask the pilot to disregard the RA

questions.explanation: The document states: 'When a pilot reports an ACAS resolution advisory (RA), the controller shall not attempt to modify the aircraft flight path until the pilot reports 'Clear of Conflict'.' (Chapter 15, 15.7.3.2)

questions.question: What is the maximum time after take-off that an aircraft experiencing communication failure in IMC should continue at last assigned speed and level before adjusting?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'unless otherwise prescribed on the basis of a regional air navigation agreement, in airspace where procedural separation is being applied, maintain the last assigned speed and level, or minimum flight altitude if higher, for a period of 20 minutes following the aircraft's failure to report its position over a compulsory reporting point and thereafter adjust level and speed in accordance with the filed flight plan.' (Chapter 15, 15.3.3 b) 1))

questions.question: What is the maximum time after which an aircraft in communication failure in IMC and under radar control should maintain last assigned level before adjusting?
questions.explanation: Chapter 15, 15.3.3 b) 2): 'in airspace where an ATS surveillance system is used in the provision of air traffic control, maintain the last assigned speed and level, or minimum flight altitude if higher, for a period of 7 minutes following: i) the time the last assigned level or minimum flight altitude is reached; or ii) the time the transponder is set to Code 7600 or the ADS-B transmitter is set to indicate the loss of air-ground communications; or iii) the aircraft's failure to report its position over a compulsory reporting point; whichever is later and thereafter adjust level and speed in accordance with the filed flight plan.'

questions.question: What is the primary objective of the short-term conflict alert (STCA) function?

options.B: To assist the controller in preventing collision between aircraft

questions.explanation: Chapter 15, 15.7.2 Note 1: 'The objective of the STCA function is to assist the controller in preventing collision between aircraft by generating, in a timely manner, an alert of a potential or actual infringement of separation minima.'

questions.question: What is the meaning of the term 'Unidentified aircraft'?

options.A: An aircraft that has not filed a flight plan
options.B: An aircraft which has been observed or reported to be operating in a given area but whose identity has not been established
options.C: An aircraft with a faulty transponder
options.D: An aircraft that is not in communication
options.E: An aircraft that is not on a flight plan route
options.F: An aircraft that has been stolen

questions.explanation: Chapter 15, 15.5.1 Note 1: 'Unidentified aircraft. An aircraft which has been observed or reported to be operating in a given area but whose identity has not been established.'

questions.question: What action should an ATS unit take when it learns that an aircraft is being intercepted in its area of responsibility?

options.B: Attempt to establish two-way communication with the intercepted aircraft, inform the pilot, contact the intercept control unit, relay messages, and ensure safety
options.C: Instruct the intercepted aircraft to squawk 7700
options.D: Request the intercepting aircraft to break off
options.E: Transfer control to the military immediately

questions.explanation: Chapter 15, 15.5.2.1: 'As soon as an air traffic services unit learns that an aircraft is being intercepted in its area of responsibility, it shall take such of the following steps as are appropriate: a) attempt to establish two-way communication... b) inform the pilot of the interception; c) establish contact with the intercept control unit... d) relay messages... e) in close coordination... take all necessary steps to ensure the safety of the intercepted aircraft; f) inform ATS units serving adjacent FIRs...'

questions.question: According to ICAO DOC 4444, what is the responsibility of States regarding the level of air traffic services?

options.A: States shall ensure that the level of ATS is appropriate and adequate for maintaining an acceptable level of safety
options.B: States shall ensure that ATS is provided only during daylight hours
options.C: States shall ensure that ATS is provided free of charge
options.D: States shall ensure that ATS is provided only by military personnel
options.E: States shall ensure that ATS is harmonized with neighboring States without exception
options.F: States shall ensure that ATS is provided only for IFR flights

questions.explanation: The document states: 'States shall ensure that the level of air traffic services (ATS) and communications, navigation and surveillance, as well as the ATS procedures applicable to the airspace or aerodrome concerned, are appropriate and adequate for maintaining an acceptable level of safety in the provision of ATS.'

questions.question: What must be included in an ATS safety management system (SMS) according to the document?

options.B: Monitoring of overall safety levels, safety reviews, safety risk assessments, and a mechanism for identifying safety enhancing measures
options.C: Only safety risk assessments for new procedures

questions.explanation: The document states: 'An ATS SMS should include, inter alia, the following with respect to the provision of air traffic services: a) monitoring of overall safety levels and detection of any adverse trend; b) safety reviews of ATS units; c) safety risk assessments in respect of the planned implementation of airspace reorganizations, the introduction of new equipment systems or facilities, and new or changed ATS procedures; and d) a mechanism for identifying the need for safety enhancing measures.'

questions.question: When must a safety risk assessment be carried out?

options.B: For proposals for significant airspace reorganizations, significant changes in ATS procedures, and introduction of new equipment or systems
options.C: Only when a reduced separation minimum is proposed
options.D: Only when a new aerodrome is built

questions.explanation: The document states: 'A safety risk assessment shall be carried out in respect of proposals for significant airspace reorganizations, for significant changes in the provision of ATS procedures applicable to an airspace or an aerodrome, and for the introduction of new equipment, systems or facilities.'

questions.question: What does the term 'reliability' mean in the context of ATS systems?

options.A: The ratio of time a system is operating correctly
options.B: The probability that a device or system will function without failure over a specified time period or amount of usage
options.C: The ability to detect system failures
options.F: The number of backup systems available

questions.explanation: The document states: 'In the context above, the terms reliability and availability have the following meanings: 1) Reliability. The probability that a device or system will function without failure over a specified time period or amount of usage;'

questions.question: What is the responsibility of the appropriate ATS authority regarding safety management systems (SMS)?

options.A: To implement SMS for air traffic services under its jurisdiction
options.B: To delegate SMS to aircraft operators
options.C: To apply SMS only to en-route services
options.D: To establish SMS only for approach control
options.E: To make SMS optional for ATS units
options.F: To outsource SMS to third parties

questions.explanation: The document states: 'To ensure that safety in the provision of ATS is maintained, the appropriate ATS authority shall implement safety management systems (SMS) for the air traffic services under its jurisdiction.'

questions.question: What should a safety review of an ATS unit include with respect to regulatory issues?

options.A: Only the accuracy of navigation aids
options.B: Completeness of ATS operations manuals, adequacy of route spacing, appropriateness of separation minima, procedures for low visibility, traffic volumes, and incident reporting

questions.explanation: The document lists under 2.5.2: 'Regulatory issues to ensure that: a) ATS operations manuals... are complete, concise and up-to-date; b) the ATS route structure... provides for: 1) adequate route spacing; ... c) the separation minima... are appropriate; d) ... procedures and measures aimed at minimizing... runway incursions; e) appropriate procedures for low visibility aerodrome operations; f) traffic volumes... do not exceed safe levels; g) procedures... for failures or degradations of ATS systems; and h) procedures for the reporting of incidents...'

questions.question: What is the objective of ATFM strategic planning?

options.A: To manage immediate traffic flow on the day of operations
options.B: To examine demand for the forthcoming season and resolve imbalances by arranging capacity, re-routing, scheduling, or identifying need for tactical measures
options.C: To issue flow control messages to aircraft

questions.explanation: The document states: 'Strategic planning should be carried out in conjunction with ATC and the aircraft operators. It should consist of examining the demand for the forthcoming season, assessing where and when demand is likely to exceed the available ATC capacity and taking steps to resolve the imbalance by: a) arranging with the ATC authority to provide adequate capacity at the required place and time; b) re-routing certain traffic flows (traffic orientation); c) scheduling or rescheduling flights as appropriate; and d) identifying the need for tactical ATFM measures.'

questions.question: What factors must be considered when assessing ATC capacity?

options.A: Level and type of ATS, structural complexity, controller workload, types and reliability of CNS systems, availability of backup, and any other factor relevant to controller workload
options.C: Only the number of aircraft in the airspace

questions.explanation: The document states: 'In assessing capacity values, factors to be taken into account should include, inter alia: a) the level and type of ATS provided; b) the structural complexity of the control area...; c) controller workload...; d) the types of communications, navigation and surveillance systems in use, their degree of technical reliability and availability...; e) availability of ATC systems providing controller support and alert functions; and f) any other factor or element deemed relevant to controller workload.'

questions.question: What are the three phases of ATFM?

options.A: Strategic planning, pre-tactical planning, tactical operations

questions.explanation: The document states: 'ATFM should be carried out in three phases: a) strategic planning...; b) pre-tactical planning...; c) tactical operations...'

questions.question: What is the maximum period for which special air-reports shall be disseminated to aircraft?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Special air-reports shall be disseminated to aircraft for a period of 60 minutes after their issuance.'

questions.question: What is the objective of air traffic advisory service?

options.A: To replace air traffic control service
options.B: To make information on collision hazards more effective than flight information service
options.C: To provide separation between all aircraft
options.F: To issue clearances to IFR flights

questions.explanation: The document states: 'The objective of the air traffic advisory service is to make information on collision hazards more effective than it would be in the mere provision of flight information service.'

questions.question: When shall an ATS unit forward an alerting message?

options.B: When an aircraft is considered to be in a state of emergency

questions.question: What is the priority indicator for distress messages on the AFTN?

questions.question: What does the AFTN priority indicator 'FF' represent?

questions.question: What does the AFTN addressee indicator 'ZQZ' represent?

options.B: Centre in charge of a FIR or upper FIR for IFR flight

questions.explanation: Appendix 3? Actually Chapter 11: 'The following three-letter designators shall be used when addressing ATS messages to ATS units: Centre in charge of a flight information region or an upper flight information region (whether ACC or FIC): — if the message is relevant to an IFR flight ZQZ'

questions.question: What is the priority indicator for a flight plan message on the AFTN?
questions.explanation: Chapter 11, 11.1.3: 'Movement and control messages (FF)', and filed flight plan (FPL) is a movement message. So FF.

questions.question: What does the filing time in an AFTN message consist of?

options.A: A six-digit date-time group indicating date and time of filing

questions.explanation: Chapter 11, 11.2.1.2.4: 'The filing time shall consist of a six-digit date-time group indicating the date and the time of filing the message for transmission with the aeronautical fixed station concerned.'

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'Logical Acknowledgement (LAM)' message?

options.A: To indicate that a clearance has been accepted
options.B: To automatically acknowledge receipt and processing of a message between ATC computers

questions.explanation: Chapter 11, 11.4.2.3.6: 'An LAM shall be used only between ATC computers.' and 'An ATC computer shall transmit an LAM in response to a CPL or EST or other appropriate message which is received and processed up to the point where the operational content will be received by the appropriate controller.'

questions.question: When shall a current flight plan (CPL) message be transmitted by an ACC to the next ACC?

options.A: Only when requested by the next ACC
options.B: For each controlled flight and for each flight provided with air traffic advisory service, in sufficient time to permit receipt at least 20 minutes before the aircraft estimates passing the transfer of control point

options.E: Only when a flight plan is amended

questions.explanation: The document states: 'A CPL message shall be transmitted by each ACC to the next ACC ... for each controlled flight, and for each flight provided with air traffic advisory service ... in sufficient time to permit each ATS unit concerned to receive the information at least 20 minutes before the time at which the aircraft is estimated to pass the transfer of control point.'

questions.question: What is the AFTN priority indicator for a message concerning a flight plan modification (CHG)?
questions.explanation: Chapter 11, 11.1.3: 'Movement and control messages (FF)' includes modification messages (CHG). So FF is the priority indicator.

questions.question: What is the phraseology to be used when a pilot is unable to comply with a clearance or instruction?
questions.explanation: The document lists under 'when a pilot is unable to comply with a clearance or instruction' the phraseology '*q) UNABLE;'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct an aircraft to change to a new frequency?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Transfer of control and/or frequency change a) CONTACT (unit call sign) (frequency) [NOW];'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to issue a surveillance radar approach?

options.B: THIS WILL BE A SURVEILLANCE RADAR APPROACH RUNWAY (number) TERMINATING AT (distance) FROM TOUCHDOWN

questions.explanation: The document states: 'a) THIS WILL BE A SURVEILLANCE RADAR APPROACH RUNWAY (number) TERMINATING AT (distance) FROM TOUCHDOWN, OBSTACLE CLEARANCE ALTITUDE (or HEIGHT) (number) METRES (or FEET) CHECK YOUR MINIMA'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to request an aircraft to squawk a specific code?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'SQUAWK (code).'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct an aircraft to stop squawking?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'SQUAWK STANDBY' for temporary suspension of transponder operation. Also 'STOP SQUAWK' is listed for termination of transponder operation. The question asks 'stop squawking' - both could be argued. But 'SQUAWK STANDBY' is the standard phrase for temporarily suspending. However, 'STOP SQUAWK' is also used. To be precise, the document has 'STOP SQUAWK' under 12.4.3.10 for termination, and 'SQUAWK STANDBY' for temporary. Since the question doesn't specify temporary, both are correct? The options only have 'SQUAWK STANDBY' as the closest. 'STOP SQUAWK' is not an option. So answer is D.

questions.question: What is the phraseology for a low altitude warning?

options.B: (aircraft call sign) LOW ALTITUDE WARNING, CHECK YOUR ALTITUDE IMMEDIATELY, QNH IS ...

questions.explanation: The document states: '(aircraft call sign) LOW ALTITUDE WARNING, CHECK YOUR ALTITUDE IMMEDIATELY, QNH IS (number) [(units)]. [THE MINIMUM FLIGHT ALTITUDE IS (altitude)].'

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'ADVISE ABLE TO ACCEPT VISUAL APPROACH' phraseology?

options.A: The controller is requesting the pilot to indicate if a visual approach is possible
options.B: The controller is clearing the aircraft for a visual approach
options.C: The pilot is requesting a visual approach

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct a helicopter to air-taxi?
questions.explanation: Chapter 12, 12.3.4.7 includes: 'l) AIR-TAXI TO (or VIA) (location or routing as appropriate) [CAUTION (dust, blowing snow, loose debris, taxiing light aircraft, personnel, etc.)];'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to ask an aircraft to report when it has vacated a runway?
questions.explanation: The phrase 'CROSS RUNWAY (number) [REPORT VACATED]' is listed under 12.3.4.9. Note also that the pilot will respond 'RUNWAY VACATED'.

questions.question: What is the phraseology for a precision radar approach elevation correction when the aircraft is slightly above the glide path?

questions.explanation: Under PAR approach (12.4.2.5.4), the phraseology includes 'SLIGHTLY (or WELL, or GOING) ABOVE (or BELOW) GLIDE PATH'.

questions.question: What does the phrase 'EXPEDITE CLIMB' mean?

options.B: Climb at the maximum rate possible

questions.explanation: The phrase 'EXPEDITE CLIMB (or DESCENT) [UNTIL PASSING (level)]' is listed under level changes, and 'expedite' generally means to do something quickly. In context, it means to climb as fast as operationally feasible.

questions.question: What is the correct phraseology to request a pilot to squawk ident?
questions.explanation: The document under SSR phraseologies: 'To request the operation of the IDENT feature: a) SQUAWK [(code)] [AND] IDENT;'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct an aircraft to resume own navigation after vectoring?
questions.explanation: Under ATS surveillance phraseologies: 'Termination of vectoring: a) RESUME OWN NAVIGATION (position of aircraft) (specific instructions); b) RESUME OWN NAVIGATION [DIRECT] (significant point) [MAGNETIC TRACK ...]'

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'FREQUENCY CHANGE APPROVED' phraseology?

options.A: The controller authorizes the aircraft to change to another frequency

questions.explanation: Under 'Transfer of control and/or frequency change', the phrase 'FREQUENCY CHANGE APPROVED' is listed as a response to a pilot request 'REQUEST CHANGE TO (frequency)'. It indicates ATC approval to change frequency.

questions.question: What is the meaning of the phrase 'TAXI INTO HOLDING BAY'?

options.B: Taxi into a designated parking area or bay off the taxiway

questions.explanation: Under taxi procedures, the phrase 'TAXI INTO HOLDING BAY' is listed. A holding bay is an area where aircraft can wait off the taxiway, typically before the runway holding point. It is a designated area to hold short of the active runway.

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct an aircraft to stop a take-off after it has commenced take-off roll?

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct an aircraft to make a three-sixty turn?

options.B: MAKE A THREE SIXTY TURN LEFT (or RIGHT) [reason]

questions.explanation: Under ATS surveillance phraseologies: 'a) MAKE A THREE SIXTY TURN LEFT (or RIGHT) [reason];' (Chapter 12, 12.4.1.5)

questions.question: What is the phraseology for an aircraft to report that it is established on the localizer?

questions.question: What is the phraseology to request an aircraft to confirm that it is RVSM approved?
questions.explanation: Under RVSM operations phraseology: 'a) CONFIRM RVSM APPROVED;' (Chapter 12, 12.3.1.13)

questions.question: What is the meaning of the phrase 'CAVOK' in meteorological information?

options.C: No significant weather, ceiling and visibility OK
options.D: Ceiling above 5,000 ft and visibility more than 10 km, no precipitation

questions.explanation: The phrase 'CAVOK' is listed under meteorological conditions phraseologies. While the document does not expand on its meaning, it is standard aviation phraseology for 'Ceiling and Visibility OK' (no significant weather, visibility 10 km or more, no clouds below 5,000 ft or MSA, no precipitation, thunderstorms, etc.).

questions.question: What is the phraseology to cancel a take-off clearance?

questions.explanation: The document states: '... to cancel a take-off clearance e) HOLD POSITION, CANCEL TAKE-OFF I SAY AGAIN CANCEL TAKE-OFF (reasons);' (Chapter 12, 12.3.4.11)

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct an aircraft to resume published speed?

questions.question: What is the meaning of the phrase 'WHEN READY (instruction)' in ATC phraseology?

questions.explanation: Chapter 12, 12.3.1.2, under level changes: '... to require action when convenient m) WHEN READY (instruction);'

questions.question: What is the correct phraseology for a pilot to report that the aircraft is established on the track between two significant points with zero offset?

options.D: ESTABLISHED ON THE TRACK BETWEEN (point) AND (point) [WITH ZERO OFFSET]

The most complete correct answer includes the points and zero offset if required.

questions.question: What is the meaning of the phrase 'CHECK STUCK MICROPHONE (frequency)'?

questions.question: What is the phraseology to request a pilot to squawk Mode C?
questions.explanation: Chapter 12, 12.4.3.11: 'To request transmission of pressure-altitude: a) SQUAWK CHARLIE;'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to instruct an aircraft to make a short approach?
questions.explanation: Chapter 12, 12.3.4.15: 'a) MAKE SHORT APPROACH;'

questions.explanation: Chapter 12, 12.3.4.16: '*g) REQUEST LOW PASS (reasons);'

questions.question: When may strategic lateral offset procedures (SLOP) be authorized?

options.B: Only in en-route airspace where lateral separation minima are 15 NM or more (offsets up to 2 NM), or in other specified conditions
options.C: At any time and any airspace
options.F: Only when authorized by the pilot

questions.explanation: The document states: 'Strategic lateral offsets shall be authorized only in en-route airspace as follows: a) where the lateral separation minima or spacing between route centre lines is 28 km (15 NM) or more, offsets to the right ... up to a maximum of 3.7 km (2 NM); and b) where ... 19 km (10 NM) or more and less than 28 km (15 NM), while one aircraft climbs/descends ...; and c) where ... 11.1 km (6 NM) or more and less than 28 km (15 NM), offsets up to 0.9 km (0.5 NM).'

questions.question: What information should the flight crew provide to ATS when a suspected communicable disease is on board?

options.B: Aircraft identification, departure aerodrome, destination aerodrome, estimated time of arrival, number of persons on board, number of suspected cases, and nature of public health risk if known
options.C: Only the number of suspected cases
options.D: Destination and estimated time of arrival only
options.F: Only the nature of the disease

questions.explanation: The document lists: 'a) aircraft identification; b) departure aerodrome; c) destination aerodrome; d) estimated time of arrival; e) number of persons on board; f) number of suspected case(s) on board; and g) nature of the public health risk, if known.'

questions.question: What is the maximum strategic lateral offset (SLOP) allowed to the right of the centre line when the lateral separation minima between route centre lines is 15 NM or more?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Strategic lateral offsets shall be authorized only in en-route airspace as follows: a) where the lateral separation minima or spacing between route centre lines is 28 km (15 NM) or more, offsets to the right of the centre line relative to the direction of flight in tenths of a nautical mile up to a maximum of 3.7 km (2 NM).'

questions.question: What is the maximum number of hours before departure that a repetitive flight plan (RPL) listing must be submitted for permanent changes?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Changes of a permanent nature involving the inclusion of new flights and the deletion or modification of currently listed flights shall be submitted in the form of amendment listings. These listings shall reach the air traffic services agency concerned at least seven days prior to the change becoming effective.'

questions.question: What is the responsibility of the operator regarding RPL changes of a temporary nature (e.g., delay of 30 minutes or more)?

options.A: Notify the ATS unit responsible for the departure aerodrome immediately
options.C: Do nothing, as RPL automatically adjusts

questions.explanation: Chapter 16, 16.4.4.2.3: 'Whenever it is expected by the operator that a specific flight, for which an RPL has been submitted, is likely to encounter a delay of 30 minutes or more in excess of the off-block time stated in that flight plan, the ATS unit responsible for the departure aerodrome shall be notified immediately.'

questions.question: What is the minimum lead time for submitting complete RPL listings for international flights?
questions.explanation: Chapter 16, 16.4.3.2: 'The minimum lead time required for the submission of such listings shall be established by the administrations concerned and published in their AIPs. This minimum lead time shall be at least two weeks.' Two weeks = 14 days. So 14 days. But option E is 14 days. However, the question says 'minimum lead time for submitting complete RPL listings' and the answer is 14 days. But option C is 7 days, D is 10, E is 14. So correct is E. But wait, the text says 'at least two weeks' = 14 days. So answer E.

questions.question: What is the maximum strategic lateral offset (SLOP) allowed when lateral separation minima are between 6 NM and 15 NM?
questions.explanation: Chapter 16, 16.5.2 c): 'where the lateral separation minima or spacing between route centre lines is 11.1 km (6 NM) or more and less than 28 km (15 NM), offsets to the right of the centre line relative to the direction of flight in tenths of a nautical mile up to a maximum of 0.9 km (0.5 NM).'

questions.question: Who is responsible for the decision to apply a strategic lateral offset?
questions.explanation: Chapter 16, 16.5.4: 'The decision to apply a strategic lateral offset shall be the responsibility of the flight crew.'

questions.question: What information should the flight crew provide to ATS when a suspected communicable disease is on board, regarding the number of persons on board?

options.A: Only the number of suspected cases
options.B: Total number of persons on board and number of suspected cases
options.D: Only the nature of the disease

questions.explanation: Chapter 16, 16.6.1: 'e) number of persons on board; f) number of suspected case(s) on board;' Both are required.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of waypoints that can be specified in the en-route data parameter of a CPDLC message?
questions.explanation: In Appendix 5, Table A5-14-1, under 'en-route data' parameter description: 'Specifies the cleared route of flight for up to 128 waypoints with positional information (route information), including for each waypoint as required, level constraint, speed constraint, required time of arrival, holding instruction and fly-by or flyover information.'

questions.question: What is the phraseology to request an aircraft to confirm its assigned level?
questions.explanation: In Chapter 12, 12.3.1.2, under level changes, reports and rates, there is no direct. But in Appendix 5, CPDLC message elements, LVLU-27 is 'CONFIRM ASSIGNED LEVEL' with format 'CONFIRM ASSIGNED LEVEL'. That is the phraseology for CPDLC. For voice, the phrase 'CONFIRM ASSIGNED LEVEL' is also standard. The document does not explicitly list it in Chapter 12, but it is implied. However, given the options, B is the most correct from the CPDLC set.

questions.question: What does the CPDLC uplink message element 'RTEU-1' instruct?

options.B: Proceed via the specified departure clearance

questions.explanation: Table A5-1-1: 'RTEU-1 Instruction to proceed via the specified departure clearance. (departure clearance)'

questions.question: What does the CPDLC response 'WILCO' indicate?

questions.explanation: Table A5-10-2, RSPD-1: 'Indication that the instruction is understood and will be complied with. WILCO'

questions.question: True or False: 'The CPDLC message element 'RSPU-1' indicates that the message will be responded to shortly.'
questions.explanation: Table A5-10-1: RSPU-1 is 'UNABLE' meaning 'Indication that the message cannot be complied with.' RSPU-2 is 'STANDBY' meaning 'Indication that the message will be responded to shortly.'

questions.question: In Appendix 5, what is the response attribute for uplink message element 'LVLU-1' (EXPECT HIGHER AT TIME)?
questions.explanation: Table A5-3-1 shows for LVLU-1 the RESP (response) column is 'R', which according to Table 14-2 means the valid responses include ROGER, UNABLE, STANDBY, etc.

questions.question: What is the CPDLC uplink message element for 'MAINTAIN PRESENT SPEED'?
questions.explanation: Table A5-5-1, Speed uplinks: SPDU-5 is 'MAINTAIN PRESENT SPEED'.

questions.question: In Appendix 5, what does the message element 'EMGD-2' represent?

options.A: Indication of an urgent situation (PAN PAN)

questions.explanation: Table A5-9-2, Emergency/urgency downlinks: EMGD-2 is 'MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY' indicating an emergency situation.

questions.question: In Appendix 5, what is the response attribute for a downlink message that requires a response and has precedence 1?
questions.explanation: Table 14-3 shows downlink response attribute 'Y' means 'Yes' a response is required, and precedence 1.

questions.question: What is the CPDLC message element for 'REQUEST DIRECT TO (position)'?
questions.explanation: Table A5-1-2, Route downlinks: RTED-1 is 'Request for a direct clearance to the specified position. REQUEST DIRECT TO (position)'

questions.question: What is the AIDC message used to indicate that a flight is being cancelled before coordination?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Coordinate Cancel message is used to indicate to the receiving ATS unit that all coordination and/or notification previously received for a flight is being cancelled.'

questions.question: In Appendix 6, what is the purpose of the 'Transfer Communication Request' message?

options.A: To indicate that the transferring unit has instructed the flight to establish communication with the accepting unit
options.B: An unsolicited request by the accepting unit to establish communication with the flight immediately
options.C: To propose transfer of control conditions
options.E: To cancel a previous transfer request

questions.explanation: Appendix 6, paragraph 2.12: 'Transfer Communication Request message is an unsolicited request by the controller in the accepting unit to establish communication with the flight on the appropriate channel immediately.'

questions.question: In Appendix 6, which AIDC message is used to update the receiving unit with current control environment data such as cleared flight level, speed restrictions, etc., before transfer?
questions.explanation: Appendix 6, paragraph 2.9: 'The Transfer Initiate message satisfies the following operational requirements: a) informs the receiving unit of the current control environment of the flight; and b) alleviates the requirement for the controller in the transferring unit to verbally provide this information...' Also 2.9 describes that it contains executive data.

questions.question: In Appendix 6, what is the mandatory content of a 'Coordinate Cancel' message?

options.A: Aircraft identification, departure aerodrome, destination aerodrome, fix, and reason for cancellation

questions.explanation: Table A6-1 shows for Coordinate Cancel: mandatory fields: Aircraft identification, Departure aerodrome, Destination aerodrome, Fix, Reason for cancellation.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the AIDC 'Coordinate Reject' message?

options.B: To indicate that the proposed coordination is unacceptable and no counterproposal is being made

questions.explanation: Appendix 6, paragraph 2.6: 'Coordinate Reject message is used to indicate that the proposed coordination, or revision thereto, is unacceptable and no counterproposal is being proposed. Any existing coordination conditions remain as previously coordinated.'

questions.question: In the model flight plan form, what does 'AFIL' in Item 13 indicate?

options.A: The flight plan is for a military aircraft
options.B: The flight plan has been filed in the air

questions.question: What equipment is indicated by letter 'G' in Item 10 of the flight plan?

questions.question: In the flight plan, what does 'STS/MEDEVAC' indicate in Item 18?

questions.question: What is the correct way to express a flight level of 330 in a flight plan?

questions.question: What is the maximum length of an aircraft identification in the flight plan?

questions.question: What does the wake turbulence category letter 'J' stand for?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 9: 'J — SUPER, to indicate an aircraft type specified as such in Doc 8643, Aircraft Type Designators;'

questions.question: What equipment is indicated by 'B1' in the surveillance equipment field?

options.A: ADS-B with dedicated 1090 MHz ADS-B 'out' capability
options.C: Mode S transponder with enhanced surveillance

questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 10 (b) Surveillance equipment: 'B1 ADS-B with dedicated 1 090 MHz ADS-B 'out' capability'

questions.question: What does the indicator 'NONRVSM' in Item 18 of the flight plan signify?

options.A: The aircraft is not equipped with RVSM and intends to operate in RVSM airspace
options.C: The flight will avoid RVSM airspace
options.E: The flight is for non-scheduled operations
options.F: The aircraft has non-standard navigation equipment

questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 18, STS indicator: 'NONRVSM: for a non-RVSM capable flight intending to operate in RVSM airspace;'

questions.question: What is the correct way to express a point at 46°20'N, 078°05'W in a flight plan route?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Item 15 (c)(2) Significant point: 'Degrees and minutes (11 characters): 4 figures describing latitude in degrees and tens and units of minutes followed by 'N' or 'S', followed by 5 figures describing longitude in degrees and tens and units of minutes, followed by 'E' or 'W' ... e.g. 4620N07805W.'

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'DOF/' indicator in Item 18 of the flight plan?

options.A: Date of flight departure in YYMMDD format

questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 18: 'DOF/ The date of flight departure in a six-figure format (YYMMDD, where YY equals the year, MM equals the month and DD equals the day).'

questions.question: True or False: 'For a flight plan submitted before departure, the estimated off-block time (EOBT) is inserted in Item 13 after the departure aerodrome indicator.'
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 13 instructions: 'THEN, WITHOUT A SPACE, INSERT for a flight plan submitted before departure, the estimated off-block time (EOBT)'

questions.question: True or False: 'The aircraft address in Item 18 of the flight plan is expressed as a six-digit octal number.'
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 18, CODE/ indicator: 'Aircraft address (expressed in the form of an alphanumerical code of six hexadecimal characters)' - hexadecimal, not octal.

questions.question: True or False: 'In the flight plan, the wake turbulence category for a LIGHT aircraft is indicated by the letter L.'
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 9: 'L — LIGHT, to indicate an aircraft type with a maximum certificated take-off mass of 7 000 kg or less.'

questions.question: What is the maximum number of destination alternate aerodromes that can be specified in Item 16 of the flight plan?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 16 instructions: 'INSERT the ICAO four-letter location indicator(s) of not more than two destination alternate aerodromes'

questions.question: In Appendix 2, Item 10, what does the letter 'R' indicate in the radiocommunication, navigation and approach aid equipment field?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Item 10, letter 'R' is defined as 'PBN approved (See Note 4)'. Note 4 indicates that the performance-based navigation levels are specified in Item 18 following the indicator PBN/.

questions.question: What is the correct way to indicate 'RNAV 5 GNSS' capability in Item 18 of the flight plan?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 18, under PBN/ indicator, the specification for RNAV 5 GNSS is 'B2'. The format is 'PBN/B2' (or with other descriptors).

questions.question: In Appendix 2, Item 19, what does the letter 'C' under dinghies indicate?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 19, D/ (DINGHIES): 'C if dinghies are covered.'

questions.question: What is the maximum number of mandatory RNAV and/or RNP descriptors that can be specified under the PBN/ indicator in Item 18?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 18, PBN/ indicator: 'Include as many of the descriptors below, as apply to the flight, up to a maximum of 8 entries, i.e. a total of not more than 16 characters.'

questions.question: For a flight plan received from an aircraft in flight, what is inserted in Item 13 instead of a departure aerodrome?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, ITEM 13 instructions: 'INSERT AFIL if the flight plan has been filed in the air.'

questions.question: What is the correct way to indicate a significant point defined by bearing and distance from a VOR in the route field?

options.B: DUB180040 (for a point 180° magnetic at 40 NM from DUB)

questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Item 15 (c)(2) Significant point: 'Bearing and distance from a reference point: The identification of the reference point, followed by the bearing from the point in the form of 3 figures giving degrees magnetic, followed by the distance from the point in the form of 3 figures expressing nautical miles. ... e.g. a point 180° magnetic at a distance of 40 nautical miles from VOR 'DUB' should be expressed as DUB180040.'

questions.question: What is the maximum number of characters allowed for a significant point coded designator?
questions.explanation: Appendix 2, Item 15 (c)(2) Significant point: 'The coded designator (2 to 5 characters) assigned to the point (e.g. LN, MAY, HADDY)'

questions.question: In ATS messages, what does the field type designator '14' represent?
questions.explanation: Appendix 3, Field Type 14 is titled 'Estimate data' and contains boundary point, time, cleared level, etc.

questions.question: What is the message type designator for a filed flight plan?
questions.explanation: Appendix 3, Table of standard message types: 'Filed flight plan message type designator FPL'.

questions.question: What is the content of a CHG (modification) message?

options.A: Only the message type and aircraft identification
options.B: Message type, aircraft identification, departure aerodrome and time, destination aerodrome and total EET, other information, and amendment fields
options.E: Message type and SSR code only

questions.explanation: Appendix 3, Field Type 3, and the composition of CHG message includes fields: 3 (message type), 7 (aircraft identification), 13 (departure aerodrome and time), 16 (destination aerodrome and total EET, alternate), 18 (other information), and 22 (amendment).

questions.question: In an ALR (alerting) message, what does Field Type 5 contain?
questions.explanation: Field Type 5 is 'Description of emergency' with elements: phase of emergency, originator of message, nature of emergency.

questions.question: What is the message type designator for a supplementary flight plan?
questions.explanation: Appendix 3, Table of standard message types: 'Supplementary flight plan SPL'.

questions.question: In a DEP (departure) message, what does Field Type 13 contain?

options.A: Departure aerodrome and actual time of departure
options.B: Departure aerodrome and estimated off-block time

questions.explanation: Field Type 13: 'the actual time of departure from the aerodrome in (a) in ALR, DEP and SPL messages'.

questions.question: In Appendix 3, Field Type 3, what is the meaning of an open bracket '(' preceding the message type designator?
questions.explanation: Appendix 3, 1.5.1: 'The beginning of the ATS data shall be indicated on page copy by an open bracket '(', which constitutes the Start-of-ATS-Data Signal.'

questions.question: In Appendix 3, Field Type 14 (Estimate data), what does the letter 'A' in the crossing condition indicate?

options.A: Aircraft will cross at or above the level
options.B: Aircraft will cross at or below the level
options.C: Aircraft will cross at the exact level
options.D: Aircraft will cross after the level
options.E: Aircraft will cross before the level
options.F: Aircraft will cross at any level

questions.explanation: Appendix 3, Field Type 14, element (e) Crossing condition: '1 LETTER as follows: A if the aircraft will cross the boundary point at or above the level in (d), or B if the aircraft will cross the boundary point at or below the level in (d).'

questions.question: In Appendix 3, Field Type 22 (Amendment), what is the correct format to amend Field Type 8 to 'IN'?
questions.explanation: Appendix 3, Field Type 22 example: 'Example of amendment of Field Type 8 (Flight rules and type of flight) to IN: –8/IN'

questions.question: In Appendix 3, what does the Alignment Function (two Carriage Returns followed by one Line Feed) accomplish?

options.B: It begins a new line on page copy to avoid exceeding 69 characters per line

questions.explanation: Appendix 3, 1.5.5: 'When the standard ATS messages are prepared in teletypewriter form, an Alignment Function (two Carriage Returns followed by one Line Feed) shall be inserted: ... whenever it is necessary to begin a new line on page copy (see Note). In such cases, the Alignment Function shall be inserted between two data elements and shall not divide an element.' The Note refers to the 69-character line limit.

questions.question: What is the responsibility of the unit providing approach control service for arriving aircraft?

options.A: To control arriving aircraft until they are transferred to the aerodrome control tower
options.B: To control arriving aircraft until they land
options.C: To provide only flight information to arriving aircraft
options.D: To transfer arriving aircraft directly to area control

questions.explanation: The document states: 'A unit providing approach control service shall retain control of arriving aircraft until such aircraft have been transferred to the aerodrome control tower and are in communication with the aerodrome control tower.' (Chapter 10, 10.1.4.1.1) Also in Chapter 4, approach control service is defined for arriving and departing controlled flights.

questions.question: What is the required action for an ATS unit when a flight ceases to be a controlled flight (e.g., leaving controlled airspace or cancelling IFR)?

options.B: Ensure that appropriate information on the flight is forwarded to the ATS unit(s) responsible for flight information and alerting services for the remaining portion
options.C: Continue to provide control service until the flight lands
options.D: Request the pilot to file a new flight plan

questions.explanation: The document states: 'In the case where a flight ceases to be operated as a controlled flight, i.e. by leaving controlled airspace or by cancelling its IFR flight and proceeding on VFR in airspace where VFR flights are not controlled, the ATC unit concerned shall ensure that appropriate information on the flight is forwarded to ATS unit(s) responsible for the provision of flight information and alerting services for the remaining portion of the flight, in order to ensure that such services will be provided to the aircraft.' (Chapter 10, 10.1.2.5)

questions.question: What is the minimum lead time for forwarding information on arriving aircraft from an ACC to an approach control unit?
questions.explanation: The document states: 'Information on arriving aircraft shall be forwarded not less than fifteen minutes before estimated time of arrival and such information shall be revised as necessary.' (Chapter 10, 10.1.3.3.3)

questions.question: According to Appendix 1, what is the required content of a special air-report transmitted by voice when moderate turbulence is encountered?

options.B: Message type designator, aircraft identification, position, time, flight level or altitude, and the phenomenon 'TURBULENCE MODERATE'
options.F: Position, time, and next position only

questions.explanation: Appendix 1, Section 1.2 states: 'Items 1 to 4 of Section 1 and the appropriate phenomenon specified in Section 3, Item 15, are required from all aircraft.' For moderate turbulence, the phenomenon is 'TURBULENCE MODERATE'. Thus the special air-report includes message type designator, aircraft identification, position, time, flight level/altitude, and the phenomenon.

questions.question: In Appendix 1, what is the definition of 'severe turbulence' for the purpose of special air-reports?

options.A: Moderate changes in aircraft attitude and/or altitude, aircraft remains in positive control
options.B: Abrupt changes in aircraft attitude and/or altitude; aircraft may be out of control for short periods; occupants forced violently against seat belts
options.D: Occasional loss of control for more than 10 seconds
options.E: Any turbulence that causes the autopilot to disconnect

questions.explanation: Appendix 1, Section 2.1, Item 9 states: 'Severe — Conditions in which abrupt changes in aircraft attitude and/or altitude occur; aircraft may be out of control for short periods. Usually, large variations in airspeed. Changes in accelerometer readings greater than 1.0 g at the aircraft's centre of gravity. Occupants are forced violently against seat belts. Loose objects are tossed about.'

questions.question: What is the correct data convention for recording a special air-report of volcanic activity when transmitted by an ATS unit to the meteorological office?

options.A: Use the message type designator 'ARS' followed by the elements in the prescribed order

questions.explanation: Appendix 1, Section 3 states: 'When receiving special air-reports, air traffic services units shall forward these air-reports without delay to the associated meteorological watch office (MWO). ... — MESSAGE TYPE DESIGNATOR. Record 'ARS' for a special air-report.'

questions.question: What is the responsibility of the pilot when a special air-report is required due to severe turbulence?

options.B: Make the report as soon as practicable after the phenomenon is encountered
options.C: Wait for the next routine position report
options.D: Report only if requested by ATC
options.E: Report only if the turbulence lasts more than 5 minutes

questions.explanation: Appendix 1, Section 1.4: 'Special air-reports shall be made as soon as practicable after a phenomenon calling for a special air-report has been observed.'

questions.question: In Appendix 4, what is the purpose of the diagram section on the air traffic incident report form?

options.A: To show the aircraft's position relative to ground obstacles
options.B: To mark passage of other aircraft relative to your aircraft in plan and elevation, including first sighting and passing distance
options.D: To show the route of flight

questions.explanation: Appendix 4, after the diagram: 'Mark passage of other aircraft relative to you, in plan on the left and in elevation on the right, assuming YOU are at the centre of each diagram. Include first sighting and passing distance.'

questions.question: According to the glossary, what is the definition of 'Acceptable level of safety performance (ALoSP)'?

options.A: The level of safety performance agreed by State authorities to be achieved for the civil aviation system in a State, expressed in terms of safety performance targets and safety performance indicators.
options.B: The minimum number of accidents allowed per year in a State.
options.C: A target set by ICAO for global aviation safety.
options.D: The safety performance of a single service provider.
options.E: A measure of safety culture maturity.
options.F: The level at which no further safety improvements are needed.

questions.explanation: The glossary states: 'Acceptable level of safety performance (ALoSP) - The level of safety performance agreed by State authorities to be achieved for the civil aviation system in a State, as defined in its State safety programme, expressed in terms of safety performance targets and safety performance indicators.'

questions.question: True or False: The 'Accountable executive' is defined as a group of people collectively responsible for the service provider's SMS.
questions.explanation: The glossary defines 'Accountable executive' as 'A single, identifiable person having responsibility for the effective and efficient performance of the service provider's SMS.'

questions.question: Which of the following best describes 'Change management' as defined in the glossary?

options.A: A reactive process to correct errors after they occur.
options.B: A formal process to manage changes within an organization in a systematic manner, so that changes which may impact identified hazards and risk mitigation strategies are accounted for, before the implementation of such changes.
options.C: The process of replacing old equipment with new technology.
options.D: A method to increase production without considering safety.
options.E: The management of financial changes in an aviation organization.
options.F: A process that only applies to changes in safety regulations.

questions.explanation: The glossary defines 'Change management' as 'A formal process to manage changes within an organization in a systematic manner, so that changes which may impact identified hazards and risk mitigation strategies are accounted for, before the implementation of such changes.'

questions.question: According to the glossary, what are 'Defences'?

options.A: The legal protections for safety reporters.
options.B: Specific mitigating actions, preventive controls or recovery measures put in place to prevent the realization of a hazard or its escalation into an undesirable consequence.
options.C: The walls and barriers in an air traffic control tower.
options.D: The financial reserves set aside for accident compensation.

questions.explanation: The glossary states: 'Defences - Specific mitigating actions, preventive controls or recovery measures put in place to prevent the realization of a hazard or its escalation into an undesirable consequence.'

questions.question: True or False: 'Errors' are defined as intentional rule-breaking by operational personnel.
questions.explanation: The glossary defines 'Errors' as 'An action or inaction by an operational person that leads to deviations from organizational, or the operational person's, intentions or expectations.' It does not specify intentionality; rule-breaking may be a separate concept.

questions.question: In the glossary, a 'Hazard' is defined as:

options.A: The probability of an accident occurring.
options.B: An action that leads to an incident.
options.C: A condition or an object with the potential to cause or contribute to an aircraft incident or accident.
options.D: The consequence of a safety risk.
options.E: A type of safety reporting system.
options.F: The mitigation strategy for a risk.

questions.explanation: The glossary states: 'Hazard - A condition or an object with the potential to cause or contribute to an aircraft incident or accident.'

questions.question: What is 'Risk mitigation' according to the glossary?

options.A: The process of eliminating all hazards.
options.B: The process of incorporating defences, preventive controls or recovery measures to lower the severity and/or likelihood of a hazard's projected consequence.
options.C: The transfer of risk to another organization.
options.D: The acceptance of risk without action.

questions.explanation: The glossary defines 'Risk mitigation' as 'The process of incorporating defences, preventive controls or recovery measures to lower the severity and/or likelihood of a hazard's projected consequence.'

questions.question: True or False: The glossary defines 'Safety' as the complete elimination of all risks associated with aviation activities.
questions.explanation: The glossary defines 'Safety' as 'The state in which risks associated with aviation activities, related to, or in direct support of the operation of aircraft, are reduced and controlled to an acceptable level.' Not complete elimination, but reduction to an acceptable level.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of 'Safety data' as described in the glossary?

options.A: A processed report summarizing accident trends.
options.B: A defined set of facts or set of safety values collected from various aviation-related sources, which is used to maintain or improve safety.
options.C: The final conclusions of a safety investigation.
options.E: A training manual for safety personnel.
options.F: A press release about safety improvements.

questions.explanation: The glossary states: 'Safety data - A defined set of facts or set of safety values collected from various aviation-related sources, which is used to maintain or improve safety.' The note gives examples like accident/incident investigations, safety reporting, etc.

questions.question: What distinguishes 'Safety information' from 'Safety data'?

options.A: Safety information is raw, unprocessed data.
options.B: Safety information is safety data processed, organized or analysed in a given context so as to make it useful for safety management purposes.
options.C: Safety information is only derived from accident investigations.
options.D: Safety information is the same as safety data.
options.E: Safety information is confidential, while safety data is public.
