questions.question: Gawadar Airport is used only for domestic flight operation.
questions.explanation: Gawadar also handles international flights.

questions.question: Passenger charges are same at Karachi, Lahore, Islamabad & Multan.
questions.explanation: Charges vary by airport.

questions.question: Landing charges for all type of aircraft are same at all airports in Pakistan.
questions.explanation: Landing charges vary by airport and aircraft type.

questions.question: A number expressing the relative effect of an aircraft on a pavement for a specified standard subgrade category is called Aircraft Classification Number (ACN).
questions.explanation: ACN is the aircraft classification number.

questions.question: AFTN address designated for DGCAA is OPHQYAYX:
questions.explanation: DGCAA AFTN address is OPHQYAYX.

questions.question: Flight Plans shall be submitted in PFIU for all flights except local flights.
questions.explanation: Flight plans are submitted to ATS reporting office, not PFIU specifically.

questions.question: Flight Plans shall be submitted at least 30 minutes before departure of domestic flights.
questions.explanation: 30 minutes before departure.

questions.question: VFR Flights shall not be operated above FL150:
questions.explanation: VFR is not permitted above FL150.

questions.question: A UNIT of horizontal speed including wind speed is called Knots.
questions.explanation: Knots is a unit of speed.

questions.question: A Unit of distance used in navigation, position reporting etc is called Nautical Miles.
questions.explanation: Nautical mile is used.

questions.question: Aerodrome operator shall review all AIS publications on receipt from AIS and after such review shall notify AIS of any inaccurate information contained therein pertaining to the concerned Aerodrome.
questions.explanation: Operator must review and report errors.

questions.question: Aeronautical Information Circular (AIC) is a notice containing information that ______ for the issuing of NOTAM or for inclusion in the AIP, but which relates to flight safety, air navigation, technical, administrative or legislative matters.
questions.explanation: AIC contains information that does not qualify for NOTAM or AIP.

questions.question: Aeronautical Information Management (AIM) system is the dynamic, integrated management of ______ through the provision and exchange of quality-assured digital aeronautical data in collaboration with all parties.
questions.explanation: AIM manages Aeronautical Information.

questions.question: Aeronautical Information Publication (AIP) is a publication issued by or with the authority of a State and containing aeronautical information of a ______ character essential to air navigation.
questions.explanation: AIP contains lasting information.

questions.question: Aeronautical Information Service (AIS) is a service established within the defined area of coverage responsible for the provision of ______ data and aeronautical information necessary for the safety, regularity and efficiency of air navigation.
questions.explanation: AIS provides aeronautical data.

questions.question: AIP Amendment is issued for ______ changes to the information contained in the AIP.
questions.explanation: AIP Amendment is for permanent changes.

questions.question: AIP Supplement is issued for temporary changes to the information contained in the AIP which are published by means of ______ pages.
questions.explanation: AIP Supplements are special pages.

questions.question: AIRAC is an acronym (Aeronautical Information Regulation and Control) signifies a system aimed at advance notification, based on ______, of circumstances that necessitate significant changes in operating practices.
questions.explanation: AIRAC uses common effective dates.

questions.question: AIS Product is an aeronautical data and aeronautical information provided in the form of the elements of the integrated Aeronautical Information Package, including aeronautical charts, or in the form of suitable electronic data ______ NOTAM and PIB.
questions.explanation: AIS Product includes NOTAM and PIB.

questions.question: ASHTAM is a special series of NOTAM, notifying by means of a specific format change in activity of a ______, a volcanic eruption and/or volcanic ash cloud that is of significant to aircraft operations.
questions.explanation: ASHTAM notifies volcanic activity.

questions.question: Magnetic Variation is the ______ difference between True North and Magnetic North.
questions.explanation: Magnetic variation is the angular difference.

questions.question: Aerodrome Reference Point is the designated geographical ______ of an aerodrome.
questions.explanation: ARP is the geographical location.

questions.question: Data Quality is a degree or level of confidence that the data provided meet the requirements of the ______ in terms of accuracy, resolution and integrity.
questions.explanation: Data quality meets data user requirements.

questions.question: Geodesic Distance is the ______ distance between any two points on a mathematically defined ellipsoidal surface.
questions.explanation: Geodesic distance is the shortest distance.

questions.question: Aerodrome Elevation is the elevation of the ______ point of the landing area.
questions.explanation: Aerodrome elevation is the highest point.

questions.question: Integrity (Aeronautical Data) is a degree of assurance that an aeronautical data and its value ______ lost or altered since the data origination or authorized amendment.
questions.explanation: Integrity ensures data has not been lost or altered.

questions.question: International NOTAM office (NOF) is an office designated by a State for the exchange of NOTAM ______.
questions.explanation: NOF exchanges NOTAM internationally.

questions.question: NOTAM is a notice distributed by means of telecommunication containing information concerning the establishment, condition or change in any aeronautical facility, service, procedure or hazard, the timely knowledge of which is essential to personnel concerned with ______.
questions.explanation: NOTAM is essential for flight operations.

questions.question: Write down the following messages in order of priority: a) DD, b) FF, c) SS. The correct order from highest to lowest priority is:
questions.explanation: Priority order: SS (distress), DD (urgency), FF (flight safety).

questions.question: The abbreviation of Filed Flight Plan is:
questions.explanation: FPL stands for Filed Flight Plan.

questions.question: The abbreviation of Delay is:
questions.explanation: DLA stands for Delay.

questions.question: The abbreviation of Modification is:
questions.explanation: CHG stands for Modification (change).

questions.question: The abbreviation of Flight Plan Cancellation is:
questions.explanation: CNL stands for Cancellation.

questions.question: The abbreviation of Coordination is:
questions.explanation: CDN stands for Coordination.

questions.question: SIGMET is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: SIGMET stands for Significant Meteorological Information.

questions.question: SKED is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: SKED stands for Schedule or Scheduled.

questions.question: RVSM is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: RVSM stands for Reduced Vertical Separation Minima.

questions.question: SALS is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: SALS stands for Simple Approach Lighting System.

questions.question: PALS is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: PALS stands for Precision Approach Lighting System.

questions.question: TACAN is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: TACAN stands for UHF Tactical Air Navigation Aid.

questions.question: STAR is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: STAR stands for Standard Instrument Arrival.

questions.question: SID is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: SID stands for Standard Instrument Departure.

questions.question: RESA is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: RESA stands for Runway End Safety Area.

questions.question: OBST is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: OBST stands for Obstacle.

questions.question: OUBD is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: OUBD stands for Outbound.

questions.question: PAPI is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: PAPI stands for Precision Approach Path Indicator.

questions.question: PANS is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: PANS stands for Procedures for Air Navigation Services.

questions.question: PAR is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: PAR stands for Precision Approach Radar.

questions.question: PATC is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: PATC stands for Precision Approach Terrain Chart.

questions.question: INSTR is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: INSTR stands for Instrument.

questions.question: LORAN is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: LORAN stands for Long Range Air Navigation System.

questions.question: ROBEX is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: ROBEX stands for Regional OPMET Bulletin Exchange.

questions.question: TORA is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: TORA stands for Take-off Run Available.

questions.question: MAPT is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: MAPT stands for Missed Approach Point.

questions.question: LDA is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: LDA stands for Landing Distance Available.

questions.question: DVOR is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: DVOR stands for Doppler VOR.

questions.question: SELCAL is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: SELCAL stands for Selective Calling System.

questions.question: SUPPS is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: SUPPS stands for Regional Supplementary Procedures.

questions.question: SSR is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: SSR stands for Secondary Surveillance Radar.

questions.question: ETD is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: ETD stands for Estimated Time of Departure.

questions.question: SPECI is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: SPECI stands for Aerodrome Special Meteorological Report.

questions.question: RNAV is an abbreviation of:
questions.explanation: RNAV stands for Area Navigation.

questions.question: Airspace around an aerodrome is to be maintained free from obstacles so as to permit the intended aeroplane operation and prevent aerodromes from becoming unusable by the growth of obstacles around the aerodromes.
questions.explanation: Obstacle-free airspace is required for aerodrome operations.

questions.question: The slope of outer horizontal surface is:
questions.explanation: Outer horizontal surface slope is not a slope; it's a horizontal surface. The correct answer is None of the above.

questions.question: The slope of inner horizontal surface is:
questions.explanation: Inner horizontal surface is horizontal, no slope.

questions.question: The slope of inner transitional surface is:
questions.explanation: Inner transitional surface slope is 33.3% or 1:3? Actually ICAO: 33.3% (1:3) or 40%? The answer key says D (Both a and c), which are 40% and 33.3%.

questions.question: The obstacle free zone must be established for:
questions.explanation: OFZ is required for CAT II and III operations.

questions.question: The obstacle free zone consists of:

options.A: Inner transitional surface, inner approach surface
options.B: Inner horizontal surface, Balked landing surface
options.C: Transitional surface, inner approach surface and balked landing surface.

questions.explanation: OFZ includes inner transitional, inner approach, and balked landing surfaces.

questions.question: An outer horizontal surface is established for use by aircraft on wide visual circuits or on arrival routes or in the Circuit, or on departure or missed approach climb-paths.
questions.explanation: Outer horizontal surface is for those operations.

questions.question: For a non-precision / non-instrument runway the conical surface, inner horizontal surface, approach surface and transitional surfaces shall be established:
questions.explanation: Those surfaces are established for non-precision runways.

questions.question: Which of the following surfaces shall be established for a precision approach runway CAT-II or III.

options.B: approach surface and inner approach surface;
options.C: transitional surfaces and inner transitional surfaces;

questions.explanation: All those surfaces are required for CAT II/III.

questions.question: Overall tracking/intersecting tolerance of Navigation aids i.e VOR, NDB & LLZ are different from each other.
questions.explanation: Each aid has different tolerances.

questions.question: Overall tolerance of a DME Fix is ± (0.46 km (0.25 NM) + 1.25 per cent of the distance to the antenna).
questions.explanation: Correct formula.

questions.question: The categories of aircraft in approach procedure designing are based on:

options.A: maximum approach speed of aircraft in the landing configuration at maximum certificated mass.
options.B: minimum approach speed of aircraft in the landing configuration at maximum certificated mass.
options.C: speed at threshold based on 1.3 times stall speed in the landing configuration at maximum certificated Landing mass.

questions.explanation: Aircraft categories are based on Vat, which is 1.3 x stall speed at max landing mass.

questions.question: Minimum Sector Altitude shall be established for each aerodrome where;

options.B: Frequency of aircraft operation is too high.
options.C: Instrument approach procedure has been established.

questions.explanation: MSA is established where instrument approach procedures exist.

questions.question: Type of reversal procedures permitted are:
questions.explanation: Base turn and procedure turn are permitted.

questions.question: A standard holding pattern is defined over a facility with inbound track 200°. Aircraft arriving over the facility on track 270° will make a;
questions.explanation: For a right-hand holding pattern, track 270° is in sector 1, direct entry.

questions.question: Optimum descent gradient during the initial approach segment is;
questions.explanation: Optimum descent gradient is 4%.

questions.question: Rate of turn of an aircraft depend on the;
questions.explanation: Rate of turn depends on bank angle.

questions.question: In sectors centered on a VOR/DME or NDB/DME, it is possible to define an additional DME arc within a sector, dividing the sector into two sub-sectors with the higher MSA in the inner area.
questions.explanation: Higher MSA is in the outer area, not inner.

questions.question: Fly-over way point is defined as a waypoint at which a turn is initiated in order to join the next segment of a route or procedure.
questions.explanation: Definition of fly-over waypoint.

questions.question: Visual approach is defined as an approach by an IFR flight when either part or all of an instrument approach procedure is not completed and the approach is executed in visual reference to terrain.
questions.explanation: Definition of visual approach.

questions.question: Final approach segment is defined as that segment of an instrument approach procedure in which alignment and descent for landing are accomplished.
questions.explanation: Definition of final approach segment.

questions.question: Minimum descent Altitude or Height MDA/MDH is defined as a specified altitude or height in a 2D instrument approach operation or circling approach operation below which descent must not be made without the required visual reference.
questions.explanation: Definition of MDA/H.

questions.question: Reversal procedure is defined as a procedure designed to enable aircraft to reverse direction during the initial approach segment of an instrument approach procedure. The sequence may include procedure turns base turns.
questions.explanation: Definition of reversal procedure.

questions.question: Fly-by waypoint is defined as a waypoint which requires turn anticipation to allow tangential interception of the next segment of a route or procedure;
questions.explanation: Definition of fly-by waypoint.

questions.question: Which are the following segments of an instrument approach procedure.
questions.explanation: All are segments.

questions.question: Name the abbreviations of following fixes associated with an instrument approach procedure: Initial Approach Fix, Intermediate fix, Final approach Fix, The holding fix, Missed Approach Point / Turning Fix.
questions.explanation: Correct abbreviations: IAF, IF, FAF, HF, MAPt.

questions.question: In precision approach procedure the MAPt should be a facility or Fix.
questions.explanation: In precision approach, MAPt is normally the runway threshold.

questions.question: Arrival routes provide an operational advantage from the en-route structure to initial approach Fix.
questions.explanation: Arrival routes connect en-route to IAF.

questions.question: A STAR should commence at a Fix.
questions.explanation: STAR commences at a fix.

questions.question: A STAR may serve one or more airports within a terminal area.
questions.explanation: STAR can serve multiple airports.

questions.question: A way point is not a Fix.
questions.explanation: A waypoint is a type of fix.

questions.question: When holding is required prior to entering the initial approach segment, the holding fix and initial approach fix shall coincide.
questions.explanation: They should coincide.

questions.question: A race track pattern may have out-bound timing 1 to 3 min.
questions.explanation: 1 to 3 minutes is standard.

questions.question: The types of reversal procedure are BASE TURN, 45°/180° procedure turns and 80°/260° procedure turns.
questions.explanation: Those are the types.

questions.question: Vat is a speed at threshold based on 1.3 times stall speed in landing configuration at maximum certified landing mass.
questions.explanation: Definition of Vat.

questions.question: The missed approach procedure shall terminate at an altitude/height sufficient to permit:

options.B: Return to a designated holding pattern;

questions.explanation: All are possible.

questions.question: Fix is either:
questions.explanation: All are fixes.

questions.question: Minimum obstacle clearance (MOC) for the final segment in non-precision approach for all aircraft is 295 ft without FAF and 246 ft with FAF:
questions.explanation: Correct MOC values.

questions.question: Obstacle clearance altitude (OCA) or (OCH) in a visual manoeuvring (circling) shall not be less than:
questions.explanation: All are correct.

questions.question: The category C aircraft falls in the range of IAS.

options.A: 121 kts or more but less than 141 kts
options.B: 121 kts or more but more than 91 kts
options.D: 91 kts or more but less than 112 kts

questions.explanation: Cat C: 121-140 kts.

questions.question: The category D aircraft falls in the range of IAS.

options.A: 121 kts or more but less than 141 kts
options.B: 121 kts or more but more than 91 kts
options.D: 141 kts or more but less than 166 kts

questions.explanation: Cat D: 141-165 kts.

questions.question: category B aircraft falls in the range of IAS.

options.A: 121 kts or more but less than 141 kts
options.B: 121 kts or more but more than 91 kts
options.C: 91 kts or more but less than 112 kts

questions.explanation: Cat B: 91-120 kts.

questions.question: The term Minimum Obstacle Clearance Altitude/Height (MOCA/H) is defined as the minimum altitude for a defined segment that provides the required obstacle clearance:
questions.explanation: Definition of MOCA/H.

questions.question: The manoeuvres associated with a parallel entry into a holding pattern (overhead the fix) are, the aircraft is turned onto an outbound heading (to a track parallel to the inbound track) for the appropriate period of time or distance; then turned left onto the holding side to intercept the inbound track or to return to the fix:
questions.explanation: Description of parallel entry.

questions.question: In final approach segment alignment and descent for landing are accomplished.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Circling approach is not an extension of an instrument approach procedure which provides for visual circling of the aerodrome prior to landing.
questions.explanation: Circling approach is an extension of an instrument approach.

questions.question: Intermediate approach segment is the segment in which aircraft configuration, speed and positioning adjustments are made for entry into the final approach segment.
questions.explanation: Definition of intermediate approach segment.

questions.question: Obstacles within a buffer zone of 9 km (5 NM) around the boundaries of any given sector of MSA shall be considered as well.
questions.explanation: 5 NM buffer is considered.

questions.question: Minimum Sector Altitude shall not apply within a radius of 46 km (25 NM) of the significant point.
questions.explanation: MSA applies within 25 NM.

questions.question: A STAR should commence at a;
questions.explanation: STAR can commence at any of those.

questions.question: The overall tracking/intersecting tolerance of localizer is;
questions.explanation: The correct tolerance is not listed.

questions.question: When in an arrival segment, DME arc is used for track guidance, minimum arc radius shall be;
questions.explanation: Minimum DME arc radius is 10 NM.

questions.question: Maximum descent gradient during the initial approach segment is;
questions.explanation: Maximum is 8%.

questions.question: The optimum descent gradient during the initial approach segment is;
questions.explanation: Optimum is 4%.

questions.question: Reversal Procedures may have;
questions.explanation: All are types.

questions.question: Time considered for establishment of bank angle of 25° in an instrument approach procedure is;
questions.explanation: 3 seconds.

questions.question: Initial Approach may be along a;
questions.explanation: Any of those.

questions.question: Length of outbound track in a reversal/race track procedure may be limited by specifying the;
questions.explanation: All methods can limit outbound track.

questions.question: When the alignment criteria for a straight-in approach cannot be complied;

options.A: Visual circling OCA shall be published on the chart.
options.B: IFR flights may be requested to carryout visual approach.
options.C: Only VFR flights should be permitted to operate at aerodrome.

questions.explanation: Visual circling OCA is published.

questions.question: In a Precision Approach procedure, Missed Approach Point shall be the facility serving the aerodrome.
questions.explanation: MAPt is typically the runway threshold.

questions.question: A holding pattern is defined over a facility with in-bound 254°. Aircraft arriving to the facility on track 080° will make a direct entry into the pattern.
questions.explanation: For a standard right-hand pattern, entry depends on sector. Track 080° is not direct.

questions.question: The flight technical tolerance resulting from flight tests may modify the overall tracking tolerance of a facility.
questions.explanation: Flight tests can modify tolerances.

questions.question: In a precision approach procedure the optimum location of the MAPt is the runway threshold.
questions.explanation: MAPt is at threshold in precision approach.

questions.question: An initial approach segment has maximum segment length of 10NM.
questions.explanation: There is no fixed maximum length.

questions.question: In a holding pattern established over a facility, out-bound timings will start after crossing the holding facility.
questions.explanation: Timing starts at the facility.

questions.question: Where track guidance is provided in a particular phase of a flight, entire area of the respective segment will be the primary area.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Holding away from the station means that the outbound track of the procedure is going away from the station.
questions.explanation: Holding away from station means the holding fix is not at the station; outbound may not be away.

questions.question: A holding pattern may have out-bound timing 1 to 3 min.
questions.explanation: Standard is 1 minute (or 1.5 min above FL140).

questions.question: In Search and Rescue, coordination point for cooperating authority while accepting responsibility.

options.A: Clear objectives, scope and scale of the delegated responsibility and service required;
options.B: Full briefing on the SAR event to the extent that it will affect the service to be provided;
options.C: Conditions and constraints on use of assets;

questions.explanation: All are required.

questions.question: The Cooperating Authority must inform the Coordinating Authority of any circumstances, if they arise where the specified service cannot be provided or needs to be varied, together with reasons;
questions.explanation: Must inform.

questions.question: There are various levels of management within the SAR system.

options.A: Overall management of SAR responsibilities by SAR Authorities;
options.B: Management of individual SAR incidents by SAR Mission Coordinators (SMCs); and
options.C: Direction of SAR activities at an incident by On-Scene Coordinators (OSCs) specifically designated by the SMC.

questions.explanation: All three levels.

questions.question: Obtaining and evaluating all information pertaining to the incident, including emergency equipment carried by the person or aircraft in distress;

options.A: Classifying the SAR incident into the appropriate emergency phase (Uncertainty, Alert/Urgency, or Distress);
options.B: Dispatching SRUs immediately, if situation warrants;
options.C: Conducting initial communications checks, if unsuccessful, making an extended communications search to obtain

questions.explanation: All are duties.

questions.question: The duties of On Scene Coordinator is to supervise/have the information about:

options.C: Number and types of assets engaged in the search;

questions.explanation: None of the above? Actually OSC duties include all those. The answer key says E (None), but that seems incorrect. I'll follow the answer key.

questions.question: Media Press Releases may include the following information:

options.A: Providing relevant information to the other SAR assets.
options.B: Implementing the search and rescue plan where required.
options.C: Monitoring the performance of other assets participating in the search.

questions.explanation: Media releases are not for those purposes.

questions.question: The response to a SAR incident usually proceeds through a sequence of five stages (Awareness, Initial Action, Planning, Operations and Conclusion).
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Upon initial notification, the notified SAR authority or ATS unit classifies the SAR incident as being one of the three emergency phases:
questions.explanation: All three phases.

questions.question: A Rescue Planner shall be responsible for devising and coordinating a rescue plan. The rescue plan shall include the pre--positioning of supplies for an airdrop to survivors and the positioning of suitable rescue facilities at appropriate locations.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Liaison Officers or advisers from other authorities/interested parties, when required, may be sought from or provided by, the aircraft operator, police, military authorities etc. and shall liaise between the RCC and their parent organizations on matters of their specialization.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: The object of search and rescue (SAR) communications is to make possible the conduct of SAR operations. Such communications must allow for:

options.A: Rapid transmission of distress messages from aircraft;
options.B: Rapid communication of distress information to the authorities responsible;
options.C: Coordination of the operation of the various SAR units; and
options.D: Liaison between controlling/coordinating authorities and SAR units.

questions.explanation: All are required.

questions.question: The distress signal 'MAYDAY' is used to indicate that an aircraft or person is threatened by grave and imminent danger and requires immediate assistance. It has precedence over all other communications. The distress message is preceded by the word MAYDAY spoken three times.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: The urgency signal 'PAN PAN' is used to indicate that the calling station has a very urgent message to transmit covering the safety of a ship, aircraft or person. It has precedence over all other communications, except distress traffic. The urgency message is preceded by the words 'PAN PAN' spoken two times.
questions.explanation: PAN PAN is spoken three times.

questions.question: The safety signal 'SECURITÉ' indicates that the station is about to transmit a message concerning the safety of navigation or providing an important meteorological warning. The safety message is preceded by the word 'SECURITÉ' spoken one time.
questions.explanation: SECURITE is spoken three times.

questions.question: The international aeronautical emergency frequency 121.5 MHz is used for aircraft and those aeronautical stations primarily concerned with the safety and regularity of flight and having equipment in the 118-136 MHz VHF band.
questions.explanation: 121.5 MHz is the emergency frequency.

questions.question: People in distress may use any possible means of alerting others to their situation. These devices range from emergency radio beacons to mirrors?
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Daylight devices are:
questions.explanation: All are daylight devices.

questions.question: Night-Time Devices are:

options.A: On land, fires are arguably the most effective night time signal that survivors may use. Fires have been sighted as far as 50 miles away, with the average range varying with the size of the fire and the absence of other light sources on the earth's surface.
options.B: Flashing strobe lights are an effective compact night signaling device available for individual survivors. Strobe lights have been sighted as far as 20 miles away with an average of 3.5 miles.
options.C: Flares, star shells and rockets have been detected as far away as 35 miles, with an average of 25 miles.

questions.explanation: All are night-time devices.

questions.question: Besides the obvious radar target of the distressed aircraft itself, IFF (Identification Friend or Foe) may be used not only to indicate distress but also to increase the detectable range by radar.
questions.explanation: IFF can be used for detection.

questions.question: Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR) is the name used to describe equipment in use by military and civil aircraft. Military mode 3 is different as civil mode A?
questions.explanation: Mode 3 and Mode A are interoperable.

questions.question: The COSPAS-SARSAT System provides following data to RCCs for activation anywhere in the world.
questions.explanation: All are provided.

questions.question: Which of the following frequencies have been authorized for use in SAR operations:
questions.explanation: All are SAR frequencies.

questions.question: In exceptional circumstances, search aircraft may be allocated a 'RESCUE' call sign followed by a three-digit number:
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Some of the more extensive and readily communication facilities available for use in SAR operations are:

options.C: Mobile phone, PTCL, AMHS and fax communications

questions.explanation: All are facilities.

questions.question: A dedicated communication aircraft should be used when communications are expected to be poor in the search area and:

options.A: HF is the only means of communication;
options.B: It is necessary to improve information feedback into the RCC;
options.C: It is necessary to improve information flow to SAR units;

questions.explanation: All are valid reasons.

questions.question: The distress phase is assigned whenever immediate assistance is required by an aircraft or person threatened by grave or imminent danger or because of continued lack of information concerning progress or position.
questions.explanation: Definition of distress phase.

questions.question: Distress alerts may be received by the RCC from various sources:

options.A: Detection by the COSPAS-SARSAT satellite system;
options.C: Direct communications from the public or the distressed aircraft; or

questions.explanation: All sources.

questions.question: When a distress phase has been transferred by ATS, the RCC should:

options.A: Initiate or continue any appropriate actions normally performed during the Uncertainty and Alert Phases;
options.B: Ensure an SMC has been appointed and that all interested parties have been informed of the incident;
options.C: Examine the detailed plans of operation for the conduct of SAR operations;
options.D: Determine the availability of SAR assets to conduct SAR operations and attempt to obtain more assets if a need for them is anticipated;

questions.explanation: All actions.

questions.question: Where uncertainty exists, evaluation is usually more difficult and time consuming because of the many factors involved.
questions.explanation: All factors.

questions.question: Search planning involves the following steps:

options.A: Evaluating the situation, including the results of any previous searching.
options.B: Estimating the distress incident location and probable error of that location.
options.C: Estimating the survivors' post-distress movements and probable error of that estimate.

questions.explanation: All steps.

questions.question: A search typically involves various stages:

options.B: Stage-2 Nominated area either side of track.

questions.explanation: All stages.

questions.question: The stage one or initial search normally consists of (IMMEDIATE RESPONSE):

options.A: Action to detect by visual search or a signal from an emergency beacon;
options.C: Coordination with Police, ATS Units and other agencies as appropriate.

questions.explanation: All are part.

questions.question: Once the search area has been determined, a systematic search for the target should be planned as per following factors:

options.A: Sweep width and Type of target
options.B: Met visibility and Type of terrain
options.C: Search aircraft speed and Fatigue factor
options.D: Cloud cover and Position of the sun

questions.explanation: All factors.

questions.question: The primary purpose of any SAR action is:

options.A: To reduce delay in landing/takeoff traffic.
options.B: The speedy return to a place for safety of the survivors of a distress situation.
options.C: To include medically qualified persons in the rescue team.
options.D: To provide SAR trainings to relevant Authorities.

questions.explanation: Primary purpose is to return survivors to safety.

questions.question: The SAR Mission Coordinator (SMC) shall not ensure that proper attention is given to the preparation and execution of the rescue effort.
questions.explanation: SMC must ensure proper attention.

questions.question: When it is known that an aircraft has crashed...

options.A: RCC shall confirm the crash site and ensure the provision of all assistance
options.B: Next of kin will not be kept informed
options.C: RCC shall not endeavor to arrange security at the crash site
options.D: Police are not responsible for securing the accident scene.

questions.explanation: RCC must confirm site and provide assistance.

questions.question: After proper and complete planning land party should....

options.A: Be taken to a locality as near as possible to the distress scene
options.B: The police will determine equipment necessary for land rescue parties.
options.C: Portable radio capable of communicating with other SAR Units should always be included in a rescue party's equipment.
options.D: In cases where all occupants of a crashed aircraft are not immediately accounted for, search for missing persons must be continued.

questions.explanation: All are correct.

questions.question: The aircraft wreckage should be disturbed to assist in the recovery of survivors.
questions.explanation: Wreckage should not be disturbed unnecessarily.

questions.question: Pakistan Maritime Security Agency (PMSA) is responsible for the coordination of surface vessels engaged in the rescue of survivors in or on the sea:
questions.explanation: PMSA coordinates surface vessels.

questions.question: When both maritime rescue units and helicopters are dispatched to the same distress scene, it is not advisable to transfer survivors to the helicopters for a more rapid delivery to medical facilities.
questions.explanation: It is advisable to transfer for rapid delivery.

questions.question: When considering use of aircraft for recovery of survivors:

options.A: The rescue aircraft and crew should not be exposed to inordinate danger.
options.B: Fixed wing aircraft should only be used when there is a suitable aerodrome or landing area near the scene.
options.C: Pilots shall attempt to land at other than prepared landing areas
options.D: Specialist advice concerning the operation shall not be obtained.

questions.explanation: None are correct; pilots should not attempt unprepared areas without advice.

questions.question: SAR Operations conclude when:

options.A: The target is located and the survivors are rescued;
options.B: If there was no distress, the beacon has been turned off;
options.C: Information is received that the target is no longer in distress;
options.D: All known persons on board are accounted for,

questions.explanation: All are termination conditions.

questions.question: The authority to end a search rests with different levels within the SAR organization:
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: When the target of a search action has been located and the survivors removed to a place of safety, the RCC shall ensure:

options.B: Next of kin are fully informed;
options.C: Arrangements made for the recovery of dropped survival equipment

questions.explanation: All actions.

questions.question: When it is determined that further search would be of no avail, the SMC shall consider recommending the suspension or termination of the SAR operation.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: When a SAR action is discontinued or a search is suspended, the RCC shall not inform all authorities, units and facilities that have been activated and/or alerted.
questions.explanation: Must inform all.

questions.question: Reopening of a suspended case should be considered if significant new information is received.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Post incident debriefs should be used to establish opportunities for improvement in the operation of the National SAR System:
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: The purpose of training for all personnel employed on SAR missions is to meet SAR system objectives by developing SAR specialists.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Formal training of SAR personnel should:

options.A: focus on both the practical and theoretical application of SAR
options.B: focus on the practical application of SAR
options.C: focus on theoretical application of SAR

questions.explanation: Both practical and theoretical.

questions.question: SAR training exercises provide:

options.A: learning experience and improve liaison and coordination skills.
options.B: help demonstrate and assess the competence of the SAR service.
options.C: reveal deficiencies that may exist in SAR plans

questions.explanation: All benefits.

questions.question: Regular conduct of joint SAR exercises between SAR Organizations should form a part of any training program.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: Contracting States shall delineate Search and Rescue Regions (SRRs) and established Rescue Coordination Centers (RCCs) and Rescue Sub Centers (RSCs) for the provisioning of SAR services within SRRs.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: States may consider combining their SAR resources into a Join RCC (JRCC), responsible for both Aeronautical and Maritime SAR incidents.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: RCC may be co-located close to a well equipped center such as Flight Information Center (FIC) or Area Control Center (ACC), so that additional communication facilities can be kept to a minimum.
questions.explanation: Correct.

questions.question: ATC Team Leader may not be assigned the responsibility to supervise RCC.
questions.explanation: ATC Team Leader may supervise RCC.

questions.question: The RCC shall be manned by each ATC Team for maintaining 24 / 7 SAR Services.
questions.explanation: Correct.

4.2 Utilization in the frequency band 108 - 117.975 MHz
